IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  3200/2026
SLP (Crl.) No(s). 8997/2026
KAPIL HABLANI                                   Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF CHATTISGARH                           Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard  learned  counsel  for  the
parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order
dated 23.03.2026 passed by the High Court of
Chhattisgarh  at  Bilaspur  rejecting  the
regular bail prayer of the appellant in
connection  with  Crime  No.  288/2025,
registered  at  P.S.  Bhatapara  (City),
District Baloda-Bazar Bhatapara (C.G.).
1

--- Page 23 ---
4. The submission of the learned counsel
for the appellant is that several persons
were made accused on allegations of online
betting. Out of those multiple accused many
have been granted bail. The bail orders of
Deepak Sablani and Gaurav Hablani passed by
this Court have been annexed. It has also
been contended that there is no money trail
found  to  the  account  of  the  appellant.
Besides,  except  a  mobile  phone,  nothing
incriminating has been recovered. It has
also been contended that the appellant is a
young student and has no serious criminal
antecedents.
5. The learned Counsel for the State-
respondent has opposed the prayer for bail
but  could  not  dispute  that  several  co-
accused have been admitted to bail under
orders of this Court. 
6. Having regard to the above, without
expressing any opinion on the merits of the
case, we are of the view that the appellant
is also entitled to be released on bail at
2

--- Page 24 ---
this stage.
7. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed.
The order passed by the High Court rejecting
the regular bail prayer of the appellant is
set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on
bail on such terms and conditions as the
trial court may deem fit to impose in the
facts and circumstances of the case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.583/2016
SRI GOPALAIAH                                  APPELLANT(S)
                          VERSUS
K. MANJUNATH                                  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. This appeal is directed against the judgment and order
of remand passed by the High Court 1 dated 10.10.2013
in Criminal Appeal No.228 of 20092. 
3. The appellant was prosecuted for an offence punishable
under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act,
18813 on account of dishonor of two cheques drawn on
Vijaya Bank, Vidyanagar Branch. One cheque was of
Rs.8,00,000/- (Rupees Eight lakhs) and the other was
of Rs.50,000/- (Rupees Fifty Thousand). Both cheques
returned unpaid with endorsement ‘insufficient funds’
in the account. The complainant served the requisite
notice of demand on the accused. Despite service of
notice, the requisite amount was not paid. As a
result, the respondent filed a complaint.
4. The defense of the accused-appellant was that though
1 1 High Court of Karnataka at Bangalore
2 2 Criminal Appeal No.228 of 2009 : K. Manjunath vs. Sri Gopalaiah
3 3 N.I. Act
1

--- Page 48 ---
the two cheques bore the signature of the drawer,
those were delivered to one Srinivas.  One was a blank
cheque  given  by  way  of  security  for  return  of
Rs.50,000/- taken from Srinivas, and the other was a
cheque of Rs.50,000/- issued to return the money taken
from him. It was the case of the appellant that those
cheques were passed on by Srinivas to the complainant
who misused the same.
5. The  complainant  besides  appearing  as  a  witness
produced the relevant documents to indicate that the
necessary ingredients of the offence punishable under
Section 138 of N.I. Act were satisfied. However, the
Trial Court acquitted the appellant by holding that
except oral evidence, no documentary evidence was
produced to show that the complainant had advanced
Rs.8.50 lacs to the accused, particularly when Section
269SS of Income Tax Act proscribes receipt of loan of
amount exceeding Rs.20,000/- by cash. It, accordingly,
concluded  that  the  complainant  failed  to  prove
existence of a legally enforceable debt. Apart from
above, the Trial Court observed that the accused
already had funds therefore needed no money. 
6. Aggrieved  by  the  order  of  the  Trial  Court,  the
complainant went in appeal.  The High Court clubbed
2

--- Page 49 ---
multiple  appeals  including  the  appeal  of  the
respondent herein (i.e., Criminal Appeal No.228 of
2009) and decided them by a common judgment and order
dated  10.10.2013.   Paragraph  17  of  the  impugned
judgment which deals with the appeal of the respondent
is extracted below:
“17.  Regarding Crl.A. No.228/09:
This appeal is directed against the
judgment in C.C. No.417/2006 dated
12.02.2009  on  the  file  of  the
Addl.Civil  Judge  (Jr.Dn.)
Devanahalli  acquitting  the
respondent/accused Gopalaiah of the
offence punishable under section 138
of the N.I. Act.  Records reveal the
complainant  sought  prosecution  of
the  respondent  on  the  allegation
that the accused had approached him
for loan of Rs.8,50,000/- in the
month of November 2005 promising to
repay the same with interest within
three months.  After expiry of the
period,  when  the  complainant
approached  the  accused,  he
voluntarily, issued impugned cheques
on 13.02.2006 for Rs.8,00,000/- and
Rs.50,000/- respectively. Cheques on
presentation  were  dishonored  for
insufficiency of funds by the bank
on 25.02.2006 through its intimation
served  on  09.03.2006.  The
complainant issued statutory notice
about the dishonor of the cheque
which  the  accused  received  but
failed to pay the amount instead the
accused issued a reply notice on
23.03.2006  contending  he  has  not
availed loan from the complainant.
He took up specific plea that the
complainant is a stranger to him.
He (accused) had issued two cheques
3

--- Page 50 ---
to one Srinivas.  Srinivas misused
the  cheque  and  without  authority
passed  on  to  the  complainant.
Therefore, he denied the liability
to  pay  the  amount  to  the
complainant.  In the trial, that
ensued,  the  complainant  tendered
evidence as PW1 and examined two
witnesses K.B. Shankar as PW2 and
Raghavendrachar as P.W.3 and relied
on impugned cheques C1, C2, the bank
endorsements C3, notice copies C4
and C5 reply notice sent by the
accused C8.  The accused tendered
evidence as DW1 and examined one
witness as DW2.  He relied on D1
sale deed and passbook D2.  The
learned trial Judge analyzing the
evidence opined that even though the
complainant has tendered evidence as
PW1 supporting his contention that
he lent money to the accused and
examined the manager of the bank –
as PW2 and 3.  The defense of the
accused that he had issued cheque to
Srinivas and not to the complainant
for  Rs.80,000/-  in  the  month  of
April 2005 has not been rebutted by
the complainant.  The learned trial
Judge also noticed from the evidence
of the accused that the accused had
sufficient money with him because he
and his brother had entered into an
agreement of sale of their immovable
property and received Rs.7,50,000/-
on  24.10.2005.   The  accused  had
further received Rs.16,00,000/- in
the month of January 2006 as seen
from D1 and D2.  Thus, the learned
trial Judge has opined since the
accused had sufficient money with
him  at  the  relevant  period  the
burden  had  shifted  on  the
complainant to establish that the
accused had borrowed money.  In para
10 of his judgment the trial judge
has opined that the complainant has
4

--- Page 51 ---
tended  ocular  evidence  regarding
payment  of  Rs.8,50,000/-  to  the
accused without any document.  The
complainant has not discharged the
burden of proof.  The learned trial
Judge further opined the accused has
established  he  is  earning
Rs.1,50,000/- to Rs.2,00,000/- from
agriculture and also from vending
milk  earns  Rs.20000/-  to  30000/-
p.m.   On  that  basis  opined  the
accused  would  not  have  borrowed
money from the complainant.  Relying
on  the  decision  in  the  case  of
Shivamurthy vs. Anantharaju reported
in ILR 2008 Kar. 4629 learned Judge
has  opined  burden  rests  on  the
complainant  and  Complainant  has
failed to prove payment of amount,
presumption under section 139 of the
N.I. Act cannot be raised.  On that
basis  the  accused  has  been
acquitted.  As rightly contended by
the appellant’s counsel the learned
trial Judge has not analyzed the
evidence tendered by the complainant
to record finding as to whether or
not  the  evidence  establishes  the
ingredients constituting the offence
punishable under section 138 of the
N.I. Act”
7. While deciding the appeal(s) the High Court framed two
questions  for  it  to  jointly  dispose  of  several
appeals. Those questions were as follows:
(a) Whether in an action under Section 138 of the
N.I.  Act  for  dishounour  of  cheque,  the
complainant  is  required  to  establish  his
financial capacity to lend money?
(b) Will not presumption under Section 139 of the
5

--- Page 52 ---
N.I.  Act  accrues  to  the  benefit  of  the
complainant  unless  the  accused  rebuts  that
presumption?
8. After considering the law laid down by this Court in
several  decisions  including  a  three-Judge  Bench
decision in Rangappa versus Sri Mohan4, the High Court
concluded that the presumption mandated by Section 139
include the existence of a legally enforceable debt or
liability and to raise that presumption, the N.I. Act
nowhere requires that complaint must contain averments
about proof of capacity of the lender. Besides above,
the High Court observed that Section 269SS refers to
the recipient of money and not the lender. 
9. Based  on  the  above  conclusions,  the  High  Court
remanded the matter for the Trial Court to decide it
afresh  after  considering  the  evidence  brought  on
record.
10. Aggrieved by the order of remand, on behalf of the
appellant it is submitted that the High Court failed
to consider that there was no good reason for the
appellant to borrow money when he had proved that he
was possessed of sufficient funds for having sold a
parcel of land. It has also been submitted that
4 4 (2010) 11 SCC 441
6

--- Page 53 ---
presumption under Section 139 of the N.I. Act is a
rebuttal presumption.  As there was no need to borrow,
and no documentary evidence of any loan transaction
was produced, the accused by denial of receipt of loan
had rebutted the presumption, therefore, there was no
justification to interfere with the order of acquittal
passed by the Trial Court.
11. Per contra, on behalf of the complainant – respondent
it has been submitted that the stand taken by the
accused was not consistent. Before the Trial Court the
stand taken by the accused was to the effect that the
cheque was issued to Srinivas in lieu of hand loan of
Rs.50,000/- taken by the accused; and, on 29 th October
2005, Srinivas’ loan was returned but he did not
return the cheque by stating that they got misplaced,
later, he handed over the cheques to the complainant,
who misused the same.  The stand in the reply to the
notice of demand was different. There it was stated
that blank cheques, without signing, were handed over
to  Srinivas  to  negotiate  a  property.   In  such
circumstances,  the  statutory  presumption  was  not
rebutted as the same could only be by leading cogent
and reliable evidence. It was submitted that once it
is proved that the cheque was drawn, and it returned
7

--- Page 54 ---
unpaid  on  account  of  insufficient  funds  in  the
account, the burden to prove that the cheque was not
issued to discharge a legally enforceable debt lies on
the drawer. Such presumption arises not only under
Section 139 of the N.I. Act but also under Section 118
(a) of the Act. In that light it was submitted that
since the trial court proceeded by wrongly putting the
burden on the complainant to prove legally enforceable
debt, the High Court was justified in remanding the
matter to the trial court for a fresh consideration. 
12. We  have  accorded  due  consideration  to  the  rival
submissions and have perused the materials on record.
13. As the order passed by the High Court is one of
remand,  it  would  not  be  appropriate  for  us  to
appreciate evidence and thereby express opinion on the
merits of the case.  Suffice to state that once the
execution of the cheque is admitted, the presumption
under Section 118 (a)5 of the NI Act, that the cheque
in question was drawn for consideration, and the
presumption under Section 1396, that the holder of the
5 5 Section 118. Presumptions as to negotiable instruments.-
Until the contrary is proved, the following presumptions shall be made:-
(a) of consideration - that every negotiable instrument was made or drawn
for consideration, and that every such instrument, when it has been accepted,
endorsed, negotiated or transferred, was accepted, endorsed, negotiated or
transferred for consideration.6 6 Section 139. Presumption in favour of holder:-
8

--- Page 55 ---
cheque received the said cheque in discharge of a
legally enforceable debt or liability, arise against
the accused. Further, any breach of Section 269SS of
the Income Tax Act, 1961 is subject to a penalty under
the provisions of that Act i.e., Section 271D. Neither
Section 269SS nor Section 271 D of the Income Tax Act,
states that any transaction in breach thereof will be
illegal, invalid or statutorily void. Therefore, any
violation  of  Section  269SS  would  not  render  the
transaction unenforceable under Section 138 of the
N.I. Act or rebut the presumptions under sections 118
and 139 of the N.I. Act, because such a person,
assuming him/her to be the payee/ holder in due
course, is liable to be visited by a penalty only as
prescribed.  Consequently,  the  view  that  any
transaction in cash above Rs. 20,000 is illegal and
void and therefore does not fall within the definition
of legally enforceable debt cannot be countenanced7.
14. No doubt, the presumption under Section 139 of the
N.I.  Act  is  rebuttable  but  mere  denial  is  not
sufficient to rebut the presumption. To rebut such
It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque
received the cheque of the nature referred to in section 138 for the discharge, in
whole or in part, of any debt or other liability.7 7 See: 2025 SCC OnLine SC 2069: Sanjabi Tari v. Kishore S. Borcar & Anr., 
para 20
9

--- Page 56 ---
presumption the accused,  inter alia , would have to
explain the circumstances in which the cheque in
question came to be issued and as to how it came to be
with the payee thereof. Circumstance that the payee
had no funds to create a legally enforceable debt of
the amount equivalent to the cheque is a circumstance,
if  proved,  which  may  help  in  dislodging  the
presumption.  
15. In the instant case, it appears, the complainant had
led evidence to show his capacity to lend. However,
the trial court did not properly consider the evidence
on record.  
16. In such circumstances, we are of the view that the
High Court committed no error in remanding the matter
to  the  Trial  Court.  The  appeal  is,  therefore,
dismissed. The Trial Court shall proceed expeditiously
bearing in mind that this complaint relates to the
year 2006. 
17. All pending applications shall stand disposed of.

                             [MANOJ MISRA]
……………………………………………………J
                                   [VIJAY BISHNOI]
NEW DELHI
JULY 16, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.13728/2025
ABHAI GARG     PETITIONER
                                VERSUS
STATE OF NCT OF DELHI & ANR.     RESPONDENTS
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. An FIR came to be registered against the appellant at the
behest of respondent No.2 herein, who are undisputedly uterine
brother and sister.
4. It is the case of the complainant that after the death of
their father, appellant had dishonestly dealt with the deceased's
bank  account  and  demat  assets,  causing  impersonation  and
fraudulently liquidating shares and withdrawing money, despite the
complainant's competing claim over the estate of the deceased
father. Hence, alleging that appellant had cheated the complainant
by misappropriating the assets of the deceased by using forged
documents, she sought for action being taken, based on which, FIR
1

--- Page 59 ---
No.92/2020 came to be registered for the offence punishable under
Sections 419, 420 and 201 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860.
5. The appellant having approached the High Court for quashing
the proceedings on the grounds that the dispute is essentially
civil in nature, did not find favor, or in other words, the
petition came to be dismissed. Hence, the present appeal.
6. This Court having noticed that the relationship between the
parties is brother and sister, by order dated 21.11.2025 referred
the  matter  to  the  Supreme  Court  Mediation  Centre.  By  the
intervention of the mediator and wisdom having dawned on the
parties, resulted in a settlement agreement being entered into
between them, which is appended to the mediation report. The terms
of the settlement as agreed upon are as under:
“9. It has been agreed between the parties that in
lieu of the value of the matured bonds, Second Party
will transfer an amount of Rs. 1,52,17,360/- towards
value of bonds and Rs. 6,14,475/- towards dividend
amount (After deducting Rs. 2,78,657/- towards taxes
paid by the Second Party) (Total Amount of Rs.
1,58,31,835/-) to the First Party within a period of
thirty days from the date of obtaining certified
copies of the Orders of withdrawal of (i) Misc. Case
No. 949/2023, (ii) Criminal Revision No. 432 of 2024,
pending before Addl. District Judge, Gautam Buddh
Nagar and (iii) Complaint Case No. 81524/2024 pending
before ACJM, Gautam Buddh Nagar.
10. It has also been agreed between the parties that
stocks/shares left behind by the deceased father (as
per ANNEXURE A) shall be divided equally (50:50
ratio). Second Party confirms and undertakes that
stocks will be transferred in the Demat Account of
First Party within a period of thirty days from the
date of obtaining certified copies of the Orders of
withdrawal of (i) Misc. Case No. 949/2023, (ii)
Criminal Revision No. 432 of 2024, pending before
Addl. District Judge, Gautam Buddh Nagar and (iii)
Complaint Case No. 81524/2024 pending before ACJM,
2

--- Page 60 ---
Gautam Buddh Nagar. For the transfer of 50% shares if
any long term capital gain tax arises, then in that
case the liability would be shared equally by both
the parties.
11. It has also been agreed between the parties that
Mutual Funds left behind by the deceased father (as
per ANNEXURE C) shall be divided equally (50:50
ratio).  Both  the  parties  undertake  to  move  an
appropriate application to the holding house for
transfer of units in the decided ratio in their
respective accounts within a period of thirty days
from signing of the present agreement.
12. Second  Party  hereby  unconditionally  waives,
relinquishes and releases any right, title, interest
or claim she may have in the residential house at C-
4/107,  Sector-31,  Noida.  First  Party  agrees  to
execute relinquishment deed/NOC/ any other necessary
document within a period of 30 days from the date of
obtaining the Succession Certificate from Ld. Gautam
Buddh Nagar Court.
The Parties acknowledge that the Second Party
has never been in possession, control, management, or
enjoyment  of  the  Residential  Property  and  is
executing  this  Settlement  Agreement  only  to
relinquish any rights, title, or interest that may
vest  in  her  as  a  legal  heir.  Accordingly,  all
liabilities, dues, charges, expenses, and obligations
relating to the Residential Property, whether known
or unknown, disclosed or undisclosed, contingent or
otherwise, and whether arising before, on, or after
the execution of the transfer documents, shall be the
sole responsibility of the First Party.
Such  responsibility  shall  include,  without
limitation,  property  tax,  municipal  taxes,
maintenance charges, society dues, electricity and
water  dues,  sewerage  charges,  mutation  charges,
conversion charges, stamp duty, registration charges,
transfer  charges,  legal  expenses,  penalties,
interest, encumbrances, claims, notices, assessments,
litigation costs, and any other dues, fees, levies,
or  liabilities  imposed  by  any  municipal,  local,
revenue,  development,  governmental,  statutory,  or
regulatory authority.
The First Party shall indemnify and keep the
Second Party fully indemnified against any claim,
demand, action, proceeding, loss, cost, expense, or
liability arising in relation to the Residential
Property.

non taxable event, being a distribution of the Estate
among legal heirs, and does not give rise to any
capital gains or income under the Income Tax Act,
1961.

Deed is absolute, final, and permanent. It shall be

6

--- Page 64 ---
7. Both parties are present, namely, the appellant is physically
present and respondent No.2 is has appeared virtually. Both the
parties admit the execution of the agreement, and they have stated
that they have voluntarily entered into the said agreement without
any force, threat, or coercion, and only after having understood
the  contents  thereof,  they  have  affixed  their  signatures
voluntarily.
8. The learned advocates who are present before the Court, in
token of having identified the parties, also have affixed their
signatures to the settlement agreement.
9. In the light of the aforesaid settlement having been arrived
at between the parties, and the same being voluntary, we see no
impediment to accept the same and accordingly, it stands accepted.
10. In view of the parties themselves having agreed for all the
disputes being resolved and continuation of the pending proceedings
would not serve any fruitful purpose. Hence, we deem it proper to
quash and/or hold that all proceedings, both civil and criminal,
pending before various Courts shall stand consigned to records as
agreed to under the agreement. Accordingly, this petition stands
disposed of with no order as to costs.
11. The parties as well as the learned advocates appearing for the
parties would fairly submit that FIR No.92/2020, which has been
referred to in paragraph No.2(i) of the settlement agreement, there
is no specific reference to it in the settlement agreement about
the outcome of the said proceedings. However, they have submitted
7

--- Page 65 ---
that the said proceedings be quashed in view of the settlement
arrived at. Accordingly, the proceedings pending in FIR No.92/2020
registered by P.S.: Connaught Place, Delhi, also stands quashed.
Both parties are directed to take steps as agreed to under the
settlement  agreement  by  filing  necessary  memos  before  the
jurisdictional Court, bringing it to the notice of the respective
Courts about the settlement arrived at, as recorded by this Court,
to enable the respective Courts to dispose of / consign the records
for statistical purposes.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

O R D E R 
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. 1976 OF 2026
1. Pursuant to our directions by order dated 03.07.2026, the
Pharmacy Council of India has filed an affidavit stating therein
the following facts and the prayers for extension:
“1.  That  I  am  presently  posted  as  Deputy
Secretary in Pharmacy Council of India who is
applicant in the above-mentioned matter and I
am  well  conversant  with  the  facts  and
circumstances of the present case and as such
competent to swear this present affidavit.
2. That I have read and understood the contents
of  the  accompanying  application/Affidavit
seeking extension of schedule containing pages
1 to and I state that the contents thereof are
true and correct to the best of my knowledge
and belief, arising out of record of this case.
3. That the present affidavit is being filed
pursuant  to  the  direction  passed  by  this
Hon'ble  Court  during  the  hearing  held  on
03.07.2026, whereby the Applicant Council was
asked to file an affidavit stating that no
further extension shall be sought after the
extension  prayed  as  per  the  present
Miscellaneous  Application  for  approval  of
colleges for academic year 2026-27.
4. That this Hon'ble Court fixed the schedule
for technical education including AICTE and
other council including Pharmacy Council by
passing judgement and order dated 13.12.2012 in
Civil  Appeal  No.  9048  of  2012  titled  as
Parshavanath Charitable Trust vs. All India
Council for Technical Education (2013) 3 SCC
385, and fixed the last date of granting or
refusing approval by the authorities concerned
should not be later than 10th April of the
relevant year, and the appeals, if any, by 30th
April of the relevant year. It has further been
observed by this Hon'ble Court that if the
appellate authority decides the matter prior to
the 30th April of the concerned year and grants
2

--- Page 70 ---
approval  to  a  college,  then  alone  such
institution will be permitted to be included in
the list of colleges to which admissions are to
be made and not otherwise. "That in the past,
this  Hon'ble  Court  from  time  to  time  has
extended  the  time  limit  prescribed  in  the
judgment dated 13.12.2012 for various academic
years on the application filed by the Applicant
herein i.e., Pharmacy Council of India for each
Academic Year till academic session 2025-26."
5. That after facing the problem from last few
year that the applicant is not able to follow
the  cut-off  date  in  such  situation  the
applicant  herein  filed  an  application  with
proposal  of  revisit  the  schedule  which  is
suitable  in  present  timeline  of  academic
session  and  counselling  schedule,  as  the
applicant came every year time to time before
this Hon'ble Court for extension of schedule
and same was granted by this Hon'ble Court in
such situation the fresh schedule is need of
hour. This Hon'ble court approved the new time
schedule which is applicable since academic
session 2026-27 on 08.09.2025 in MA No. 1409 of
2025 filed in CA No. 9048 of 2012 
6. That vide order dated 06.02.2026 passed in

extended the last date of filing of SIF till
27.02.2026 for the existing institutes as the
present schedule is applicable first time due
to this reason many existing institutes are
failed to submit their SIF within stipulated
time.
7. That it is respectfully submitted that,
despite the Applicant/Pharmacy Council of India
(PCI) making diligent and timely efforts to
complete the approval process, the same has
been delayed due to factors beyond its control,
including delayed submission of the Standard
Inspection Form (SIF) by certain institutions,
non-availability of inspectors, refusal by some
institutions to permit inspection at the first
opportunity,  conflicting  schedules  affecting
the deputed inspectors, and delays in receipt
of requisite documents. Further, the Applicant
is  constrained  by  limited  resources  while
processing an exceptionally large number of
applications,  each  requiring  scrutiny,
inspection,  consideration  by  the  Executive
Committee, and final decision. Owing to these
3

--- Page 71 ---
unavoidable  administrative  and  logistical
constraints, and the practical impossibility of
engaging  and  training  additional  manpower
within  such  a  short  period,  several
institutions are yet to complete the prescribed
approval process, making it impossible for the
Applicant to conclude the approval process for
the Academic Session 2026-2027 by the cut-off
date of 30.06.2026.
8. That the inspection of the institutions done
by the teachers who are doing additional to
their regular duty and same is conducted only
one day in a week and there is limited number
of experienced teachers who can conduct the
inspection.

order:-
“ 1. The petitioner challenges the
judgment and order dated 28-04-2026
in CRMM No. 3270/2026(O&M) titled
“Deepak Kumar vs. State of Punjab”
passed by the High Court of Punjab &

1

--- Page 78 ---
3.  Mr.  Siddhant  Sharma,  learned

behalf  of  the  State  of  Punjab.
Hence, the formal service of notice
on the respondent-State is waived.
4.  Learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner to serve a copy of the
present  petition  to  the  learned
counsel  for  the  respondentState
within a period of one week.
5. Let counter affidavit be filed
within two weeks. Rejoinder thereto,
if any, be filed within one week
thereafter.
6.  In  the  event  of  arrest  in
connection with F.I.R. No.196/2025
dated  16.12.2025,  registered  at
P.S.Focal  Point,  District  Police
Commissionerate,  Ludhiana,  the
petitioner shall be released on bail
by  the  Investigating/Arresting
Officer on such terms and conditions

fair and reasonable.
7. However, in the attending facts,
one  of  the  conditions  shall
necessarily  be  that  if  the
petitioner possesses a Passport, the

Competent Authority/Court, until the

2

--- Page 79 ---
directed otherwise.

available before the Investigating

good  conduct  and  not  attempt  to
influence any of the witnesses in
any manner till the completion of
the trial. ”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.

which it was allegedly committed.

dated 15.05.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
3

--- Page 80 ---
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10

complete in all respects.

Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment/order dated 28.04.2026 is set
aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.  12212/2026
SURENDER ..... APPELLANT
              VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA ..... RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant who has been tried for an offense
came to be convicted under Sections 363, 366, 506
of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and Section 6 of
the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences
Act, 2006 Act and sentenced to 10 years of
rigorous imprisonment.
4. Being aggrieved by the same, an appeal being
CRA-S-1757 of 2022 has been filed and the same is
1

--- Page 87 ---
pending  before  the  jurisdictional  High  Court.
Successive applications (5 numbers) filed before
the High Court for suspension of sentence and
grant of bail have been rejected. Hence, the
appellant is before this Court.
5. Having heard the learned advocates appearing
for the parties, it emerges from the records that
the alleged incident took place in the year 2017,
and on the ground that subsequently, she (victim)
was again abducted on 10.01.2020, a complaint came
to  be  lodged  on  29.01.2020  resulting  in  FIR
No.08/2020 being registered for the aforestated
offenses and tried, which culminated in conviction
and sentence.
6. The  appellant  has  been  in  custody  since
January, 2020 till date, and as per the custody
certificate,  the  total  period  of  sentence
undergone  including  remission  is  7  years,  2
months, 25 days. In other words, he has undergone
70% of the sentence imposed on him.
2

--- Page 88 ---
7. That apart, there are various grounds which
have been urged in the appeal now pending before
the  High  Court,  which  includes  the  dispute
relating to the age of the victim as well as the
DNA report. The alleged discrepancies with regard
to the birth certificate at Exhibit-P29 is also an
issue which will have to be examined by the
appellate court.
8. The learned counsel appearing for the State
has contended that as and when the matter has been
listed before the jurisdictional High Court, the
appellant has sought for adjournment, and the
delay in disposal of the criminal appeal is to be
laid at the doors of the appellant himself.
9. Though several contentions have been raised
in the present appeal, we do not propose to go
into the same as it is likely to prejudice the
rights of either of the parties. Hence, leaving
open all the questions to be adjudicated in the
pending criminal appeal, we are of the considered
view that present appeal deserves to be allowed by
3

--- Page 89 ---
granting suspension of sentence and granting of
bail.
10. Accordingly, we allow this appeal and suspend
the sentence imposed on the appellant and direct
him to be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the jurisdictional Court may impose.
We  make  it  clear  that  in  the  event  of  the
undertaking  now  given  by  the  learned  counsel
appearing for the appellant that on the next date
which has been fixed for final disposal of the
criminal appeal, the counsel appearing on behalf
of  the  appellant-accused  would  address  the
arguments without fail, which is being placed on
record and failure to address the argument, the
jurisdictional High Court would be at liberty to
discharge the counsel and appoint either a counsel
from the Legal Aid or appoint an amicus and
proceed to hear the matter and dispose of the
appeal expeditiously. With these observations, the
appeal stands allowed.
4

--- Page 90 ---
11. Pending applications, if any, stand consigned
to records.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 15, 2026.

M. A. No. 1770/2026       1  

--- Page 94 ---
                   Mr. Shailesh Madiyal, Adv.

                   Mr. Vinayak Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Mukesh Kumar Maroria, AOR  
    UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the 
following
                    O R D E R
Miscellaneous Application No. 2040/2026
1. Read the order dated 24.11.2025 passed in
SLP(Crl)  No.  15849/2025.  The  Court,  while
dismissing the Special Leave Petition in Paragraph
‘5’ has observed as follows:
“5. It is made clear that all
parties would cooperate with the
trial  and  the  prosecution  will
ensure that the material witnesses
particularly,  two  lady  witnesses
are examined at the earliest. In
the  event,  the  trial  is  not
completed  within  the  aforesaid
period, it will be open for the
petitioner  to  renew  his  bail
application.”
 
2. The Applicant in the present application is
the Petitioner in the Special Leave Petition, and
M. A. No. 1770/2026       2  

--- Page 95 ---
the Petitioner/Applicant prays for the following
reliefs:-
“(A) grant regular bail to the
Applicant in connection with the
FIR No.148/2022 dtd. 22.07.22 u/s
370, 120B IPC & 3, 4, 5 of the ITP
Act of PS Crime Branch(South), New
Delhi; and 
(B) pass any such order as this
Hon’ble Court may deem fit in the
facts  and  circumstances  of  this
case.”
3. The Petitioner/Applicant is the Accused No. 1
in FIR No. 148 of 2022, a case registered under
Sections  3,  4  and  5  of  the  Immoral  Traffic
(Prevention) Act, 1956, read with Sections 370 and
120-B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860. The prayer
of  the  Petitioner/Applicant  for  the  grant  of
regular bail was rejected by both the Sessions
Court and the High Court, leading to the filing of
the Special Leave Petition read above.
4. We  have  granted  liberty  to  the
M. A. No. 1770/2026       3  

--- Page 96 ---
Petitioner/Applicant to renew the request for the
grant of bail if the trial is not completed within
six months. Availing of the liberty granted, the
present application is moved in this Court.
5. An  objection  has  been  taken  on  the
maintainability of the present application in this
Court by the Respondent(s), without prejudice to
the objections the Respondent(s) have against the
main prayer. The objection of the respondent(s) is
noted. We are of the view that the order dated
24.11.2025 has not specified the Court before
which a request for renewal of bail should can be
made by the Petitioner/ Applicant. Therefore, the
Petitioner/Applicant's  understanding  that  an
application  can  be  moved  in  this  Court  is
appreciated and accepted. Consequently, we propose
to  examine  the  merits  of  the  case  of  the
Petitioner/Applicant.
6. The  Petitioner/Applicant  was  arrested  on
22.07.2022.  The  charge-sheet  was  filed  on
21.09.2022. The Petitioner/Applicant has been in
M. A. No. 1770/2026       4  

--- Page 97 ---
continuous incarceration for the past four years.
The  prosecution  has  listed  83  witnesses  for
examination in the ongoing sessions. As of the
date,  four  witnesses  have  been  examined,  and
examination of another witness is in progress as
the order is being dictated in the Court.
7. We have taken note of the evidence of the
victims captioned as ‘ZK’ and ‘TN’. In the very
same case, another accused, namely ‘Rajbeer’, has
been granted anticipatory bail by the High Court
of Delhi, which is placed as Annexure A-8 in the
Miscellaneous Application.
8. Taking note of continued incarceration, the
role assigned to the Petitioner/Applicant, the
statements so far recorded, and the possibility of
completing the trial within a reasonable time, we
are of the view, the prayer for regular bail is
considered. We are persuaded principally on the
grounds of incarceration of Petitioner for over
four years, and the rest of the witnesses are
official or other private persons. The examination
M. A. No. 1770/2026       5  

--- Page 98 ---
of available victims is completed for the grant of
regular bail to Petitioner.
9. The order of this court shall not be treated
as a case available for consideration on parity
for the other accused facing trial in the same
matter.
10. For  the  above  reasons,  the  Miscellaneous
Application is allowed. The Petitioner/Applicant
is enlarged on bail, subject to the conditions
that are imposed by the Sessions Court.
11. In addition to the above, we direct that the
Petitioner/Applicant continue to cooperate with
the ongoing sessions and avoid all unnecessary
adjournments.
Miscellaneous Application No. 1770/2026
1. The letter dated 14.05.2026 of the learned
Presiding Officer is perused. For the reasons
stated therein, the time granted by this Court
vide  order dated 24.11.2025 is extended by six
more months.
M. A. No. 1770/2026       6  

--- Page 99 ---
2. All efforts are made by the Trial Court to
complete the trial in the meantime.
3. The Miscellaneous Application stands ordered.
4. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.999/2015
THE STATE OF HIMACHAL PRADESH                  APPELLANT(S)
                           VERSUS
DHARAM SAIN                                   RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
 
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. This appeal is against the judgment and order of the
High  Court  of  Himachal  Pradesh  at  Shimla  dated
18.12.2014 in Criminal Appeal No.28/2011 whereby the
High Court allowed the appeal of the respondent and
thereby set aside the order of the Court of Session
convicting the respondent under Sections 376 and 506
of the Indian Penal Code, 1860.
3. In brief, the prosecution case is that on 04.04.2007,
the complainant (father of the victim) lodged a report
alleging that on 03.04.2007 his daughter (the victim),
aged 11 years, complained to him that on 31.03.2007,
during daytime, when no family member was present in
the house, the accused came and sexually assaulted her
and had extended threats to her not to report the
1

--- Page 101 ---
incident.  It was alleged that the above information
came to be received from the victim, when the victim
appeared not well and was asked as to what happened.
After registration of FIR medical examination of the
victim took place.
4. In the trial, 16 witnesses were examined by the
prosecution.  The accused denied the allegations and
claimed innocence. The Trial Court by relying upon the
statement of the prosecution witnesses convicted the
respondent.
5. On respondent’s appeal, the High Court examined the
evidence in detail and found that the prosecution case
was not reliable to sustain conviction. The High Court
noticed that there was a four days’ delay in reporting
the  incident  by  the  victim  to  her  parents;  and
thereafter there was further delay of more than 12
hours  in  lodging  the  FIR.  Besides  the  medical
examination report of the victim was inconclusive to
prove that the victim was subjected to sexual assault.
Apart  from  that  there  was  no  corroboration  from
forensic evidence.  The High Court also noticed that
there  was  discrepancy  in  the  evidence  regarding
handing over of garments which the victim wore on the
day of the incident.  The High Court found that in
2

--- Page 102 ---
fact the prosecutrix did not present the salwar which
she was wearing on the fateful day for forensic
examination.  Based on its analysis and evaluation,
the High Court opined that the prosecution had failed
to bring home the charge beyond reasonable doubt.
6. Aggrieved by the order of acquittal, the State has
filed this Appeal.
7. The learned counsel for the State has submitted that
for an offence of rape it is not the requirement of
law that there should be a complete rupture of the
hymen. Even partial rupture, as found during medical
examination,  though  healed,  is  sufficient  to
corroborate  the  allegations  of  sexual  assault.
Besides, there was no reason for a false implication.
In the circumstances, once the victim had supported
the prosecution case and her testimony was broadly
corroborated by the testimony of her parents, and the
medical report did not rule out sexual assault on her,
it was not a case where the High Court should have
reversed the conviction recorded by the Trial Court.
8. Per contra, the learned counsel representing the first
respondent submitted that the view taken by the High
Court is based on appreciation of the evidence on
record and is a possible view having no perversity.
3

--- Page 103 ---
Therefore, the rule that there should be no reversal
of acquittal merely because another view is possible
would apply. It has also been submitted that the High
Court  had  considered  the  medical  evidence,  the
statement of the doctors to record a finding that
there was no conclusive evidence of sexual assault.
Besides the nature of tear in hymen found during
medical examination was, as per doctor’s evidence,
possible without intercourse and most importantly it
was  healed  thereby  ruling  out  any  recent  sexual
assault  as  alleged.  Further,  there  was  delay  in
reporting  the  incident,  which,  in  the  facts  and
circumstances of the case, had a material bearing on
the overall assessment of the prosecution case. In
such circumstances, it is submitted, this is not a
case where a well-considered judgment of acquittal be
reversed, particularly, when there is no perversity in
the reasoning of the High Court.
9. We  have  accorded  due  consideration  to  the  rival
submissions and have perused the materials available
on record.  No doubt, mere delay may not be fatal for
the prosecution particularly in an offence of sexual
assault.  However, each case is to be decided on its
own facts, and if the facts so warrant delay may have
4

--- Page 104 ---
a material bearing in determining whether prosecution
has succeeded in bringing home the charge.  Here the
doctors  despite  external  and  internal  medical
examination of the victim could not find signs of
sexual assault on the victim. In such circumstances,
when there was no reason not to report promptly, delay
assumed  importance.  Ordinarily,  where  a  child  is
sexually exploited by someone in the family or in
whose care and custody the child is placed, delay can
be ignored. However, here, the accused was not part of
the family, and the victim was throughout with her
parents, and therefore, delay cannot be ignored while
evaluating  the  evidence.  Even  non-production  of
garment for forensic examination may not be fatal to
the prosecution. However, where the medical evidence
is inconclusive and there is delay in lodging the FIR,
lack of forensic evidence assumes importance, though
the Court may, on its own assessment of the entire
evidence, arrive at a conclusion that the accused is
guilty.  But,  if  the  court  takes  a  view  to  the
contrary, based on its own assessment of the entire
evidence,  its  conclusion  being  one  of  the  views
possible cannot be called perverse as to warrant
reversal of acquittal.
5

--- Page 105 ---
10. In our view, the view taken by the High Court is a
possible view and does not suffer from perversity to
warrant an interference. Besides, on the date when the
incident  had  occurred,  there  was  no  benefit  of
presumption as is available now, under the Protection
of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (POCSO).
11. Thus,  having  regard  to  the  overall  facts  and
circumstances of the case and the detailed reasons
recorded by the High Court, we are of the view that it
is not a case where the judgment of acquittal is
liable to be reversed. 
12. Consequently, the appeal is dismissed.
13. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
……………………………………………………J
                             [MANOJ MISRA]
……………………………………………………J
                                   [VIJAY BISHNOI]
NEW DELHI
JULY 16, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
ORIGINAL CIVIL/CRIMINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL)  NO.474/2025
SWITI                                         Petitioner(s)
                           VERSUS
VIKAS KUMAR & ORS.                            Respondent(s)
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO. 161/2025 
WITH
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) No. 517/2026
O R D E R
1. These three petitions are filed by wife of Vikas
Kumar  seeking  transfer  of  three  pending  cases
namely; 
(i) Summons Criminal Case No.3046 of 2023 titled as
Smt. Switi vs. Vikas Kumar & Ors. filed under
Section 12 of the Domestic Violence Act, 2005
pending before the Court of A.C.J.M., Meerut, Uttar
Pradesh. Transfer is sought to a Court of competent
jurisdiction at North West, Rohini District Court,
Delhi;
(ii) Criminal Case No.287 of 2022 titled as “Smt.
1

--- Page 115 ---
Switi & Anr. vs. Vikas Kumar’ filed under Section
125 of the Cr.P.C., 1979 (Now 144 B.N.S.S.) pending
in the Learned Additional Judge, Family Court,
Meerut, Uttar Pradesh.  Transfer is sought to the
Court of Principal Judge, Family Court, North West,
Rohini, Delhi; and 
(iii)  Divorce Petition being Case No.609 of 2022
titled “Vikas Kumar vs. Smt. Sweeti” under Section
13 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 pending in the
Court of learned Principal Judge, Family Court,
Ghaziabad (U.P.).  Transfer is sought to the Court
of competent jurisdiction at North-West, Rohini
District Court, Delhi.
2. We  were  informed  that  in  respect  of  Divorce
Petition being Case No.609 of 2022, the petitioner
had filed a Transfer Application (Civil) No.171 of
2023 before the High Court at Allahabad.  In that
transfer application, by an interim order further
proceedings were stayed.  In those circumstances,
we gave liberty to the petitioner to withdraw the
said transfer petition. Pursuant to the aforesaid
liberty, the petitioner has withdrawn the Transfer
Application No.171 of 2023.  Copy of the withdrawal
order dated 01.05.2026 passed by the High Court has
2

--- Page 116 ---
been brought to our notice. Consequently, we can
consider the prayer for transfer of the divorce
proceedings as well.
3. As we find that the husband of the petitioner,
namely, Vikas Kumar, is currently in Delhi Police
Service, and Ghaziabad and Meerut are districts
adjoining Delhi, the request for transfer of all
proceedings to competent courts in Delhi appears
reasonable.  Therefore, we deem it appropriate to
allow these transfer petitions.  
4. Consequently, the pending proceedings, namely, (i)
Summons Criminal Case No.3046 of 2023 titled as
Smt. Switi vs. Vikas Kumar & Ors.; (ii) Criminal
Case No.287 of 2022 titled as “Smt. Switi & Anr.
vs. Vikas Kumar’ filed under Section 125 of the
Cr.P.C., 1979 (Now 144 B.N.S.S.) pending in the
Courts of Additional District Judge and Family
Court,  Meerut,  Uttar  Pradesh  respectively,  and
Divorce Petition being Case No.609 of 2022 titled
“Vikas Kumar vs. Smt. Sweeti”, under Section 13 of
the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, pending before the
Principal Judge, Family Court, Ghaziabad (U.P.),
are hereby transferred to competent court(s) having
jurisdiction to try such nature of cases located in
3

--- Page 117 ---
North-West, Rohini District Court, Delhi.
5. The transferor Court shall transmit the entire
records of the cases forthwith to the transferee
Court. 
6. For  the  convenience  of  the  parties,  video
conferencing  platform  shall  be  afforded  if  so
requested by the parties.
7. The Transfer Petitions are allowed in the above
terms. 
8. Pending application(s), if any shall stand disposed
of.

ITEM NO.9             COURT NO.11               SECTION II
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
TRANSFER PETITION (CRIMINAL)  NO.474/2025
SWITI                                         Petitioner(s)
                        VERSUS

Upon hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                               O R D E R
1. Pursuant to the order dated 16.04.2026, Ms. Vibha Datta
Makhija, learned senior counsel ( amicus curiae), has now
circulated the ‘Operation Modalities’ seeking compliance
with the directions issued by this Court.
2. We request all the learned counsel, to again give their
suggestions to such proposal and also, if so required,
interact with the officers of the respective institutions.
In fact, we direct a meeting amongst all the stakeholders
through a virtual platform be convened, and the outcome
thereof be conveyed to this Court.
3. Needless to add, the meeting shall be convened by the
Member Secretary of the National Legal Services Authority
(NALSA) at the earliest, not later than three (03) weeks
from today.
4. List on 09.09.2026.
5. The Registry to communicate a copy of this order to all
concerned for necessary compliance.

MD. FARUKH                   APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH & ANR. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The  appellant  challenges  the
judgment/order  dated  07.05.2026  in
M.C.R.C. No.3622/2026 passed by the High
Court of Chhattisgarh at Bilaspur.
3. The  appellant  was  arrested  in
connection  with  Crime  No.173  of  2025
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.11422 of 2026          1

--- Page 127 ---
registered at Police Station City Kotwali,
District Raigarh, Chhattisgarh.
4. Having heard learned counsel for the
parties and perused the material placed on
record, we are of the considered view that
the appellant has made out a case for
interference  with  the  impugned
judgment/order dated 07.05.2026.
5. Considering  the  totality  of
circumstances and also the fact that the
appellant  has  been  in  custody  since
20.02.2026, we are inclined to grant bail
to the appellant on such terms as may be
fixed  by  the  Trial  Court.  Ordered
accordingly.
6. To  comply  with  this  order,  the
appellant  shall  be  produced  before  the
Trial Court forthwith.
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.11422 of 2026          2

--- Page 128 ---
7. Learned  counsel  for  the
State/Prosecution  undertakes  to  produce
all the witnesses on such dates as may be
fixed by the Trial Court. The trial is
expedited.
8. The appellant shall fully cooperate
during the investigation/trial; shall not
misuse the liberty in any manner; shall
extend complete cooperation in the trial
of the instant case; and shall not seek
any  unnecessary  adjournment.  Should  the
trial not progress on account of the non-
cooperation of the appellant, it shall be
open  for  the  Trial  Court  to  take  all
steps, including cancellation of bail.
9. If the appellant possesses a passport,
the same shall be surrendered before the
Competent  Authority/Court  until  the
Crl.A. @ SLP (Crl.) No.11422 of 2026          3

--- Page 129 ---
conclusion of the trial or until directed
otherwise.
10. The impugned judgment and order dated
07.05.2026 shall stand set aside.
11. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
WRIT PETITION (CRL.) NO.99/2026
LIAKAT ALI                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF JAMMU AND KASHMIR          Respondent(s)
O R D E R
This is a writ petition seeking to invoke our
powers under Article 32 of the Constitution of
India, 1950.  The prayer in the writ petition is
for the grant of bail, as the petitioner has been
under incarceration for a period of more than nine
years and two months. In short, the grievance of
the petitioner is that the trial is proceeding at
a  snail’s  pace,  for  no  fault  of  his.   The
petitioner’s  bail  application  has  attained
1

--- Page 157 ---
finality in the year 2024.  Even thereafter, there
is no substantial progress in the trial, since as
of yet only 12 out of 30 prosecution witnesses
have been examined before the Trial Court.
Learned counsel appearing for the petitioner
further submits that the petitioner was a young
boy at the time of the alleged occurrence.
The charge against the petitioner is on the
basis of circumstantial evidence. The principles
governing  the  presumption  of  innocence  of  an
accused and bail is the rule and jail is exception
have to be considered.
Learned counsel appearing for the respondent-
State  submits  that  nothing  prevented  the
petitioner from filing a fresh bail application,
as the offences alleged against him are punishable
under Sections 302, 382, 201 read with Section 34
of the Ranbir Penal Code, 1989 and there is no
need for interference.
In the normal course, we would not have
2

--- Page 158 ---
entertained this petition.
However, the continued delay in trial and
prolonged  incarceration  shocks  our  judicial
conscience. The petitioner was a teenager at the
time  of  the  alleged  occurrence  and  has  been
charged with the commission of murder based on
circumstantial  evidence.  He  has  been  under
incarceration for a period of nine years and two
months, for no fault on his part.  At this rate,
the trial is likely to take further time.   When
the  accused  is  under  incarceration,  it  is
incumbent  upon  the  Court  and  the  prosecuting
agency to expedite the conduct of the trial.
Considering the facts of the case, we are inclined
to invoke our powers under Article 32 of the
Constitution of India by granting bail to the
petitioner, for which appropriate conditions will
have to be imposed by the concerned Trial Court.
Ordered accordingly.
Accordingly,  the  Writ  Petition  stands
3

--- Page 159 ---
allowed.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.  
...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026.
  
4

--- Page 160 ---

--- Page 162 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.           OF 2026
(@SLP(CRL.) NO.4521 OF 2026)
SIMRANJIT SINGH LAMBA ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF  PUNJAB ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3.  Appellant,  who  is  arraigned  as  an  accused  in  FIR
No.281/2025  registered for  the offences  punishable under
Sections 316(4) and 318(4) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita,
2023 (BNS), is before this Court for grant of anticipatory
bail as the Courts below have refused the said prayer.
4. It is the case of the prosecution that appellant, while
working as a manager and in control of the complainant’s
firms’ financial records, got excess electroplating work done
from outside firms, made false entries in the books of
1                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.4521 OF 2026

--- Page 163 ---
accounts and records, and retained the excess amount to the
tune of Rs.40 Lakhs received, thereby amounting to criminal
breach of trust and cheating to the company where he worked.
It  was  further  alleged  in  the  complaint  that  the
discrepancies allegedly found between the payments actually
received from outside firms like S J Auto Industry, K P Cycle
Industry and the amounts recorded by the appellant in the
firm’s register in his own handwriting for the months of
January, May, June, July, 2025, there were discrepancies with
regard to electroplating of cycle parts on a labour rate
basis and this has caused financial loss to the complainant.
The said complaint came to be registered in the aforesaid
FIR.
5. Learned counsel appearing for the appellant would contend
that pursuant to the interim protection granted by this
court, appellant has been appearing before the jurisdictional
Investigating Officer (IO) as and when called upon and as
such his custodial interrogation is not warranted. Whereas
the learned counsel appearing for the State as well as for
the complainant would vehemently oppose grant of anticipatory
bail by contending that appellant is not fully cooperating
2                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.4521 OF 2026

--- Page 164 ---
with the investigation and is not providing the records,
which are said to be in his custody and thus, he is not
entitled for the discretionary relief of anticipatory bail
and they pray for the rejection of the same.
6.  Having  heard  the  learned  counsels  appearing  for  the
parties and on perusal of the records, it would clearly
indicate that custodial investigation, is sought to discover
the  modus operandi . However, considering the material on
record  which  is  the  basis  for  conducting  investigation,
custodial interrogation may not be warranted. It cannot be
gainsaid by the prosecution that the accused is required to
answer in the manner in which the IO require to elicit the
answers from accused. It is needless to state that the IO
would be at liberty to confront the appellant with such
material that has come to his custody during investigation
while interrogating the appellant.
7. Hence, we are of the considered view that if strict
conditions  are  imposed  for  release  of  the  appellant  on
anticipatory bail, it would suffice to allay the apprehension
of the prosecution.
3                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.4521 OF 2026

--- Page 165 ---
8. Hence, the appeal is allowed. Impugned order is set aside.
Appellant is ordered to be released on anticipatory bail by
the IO on such terms and conditions as he deems fit taking
into consideration the observations made hereinabove. It is
needless to say that appellant shall appear before the IO as
and when called upon and cooperate with the investigation and
shall also appear before the jurisdictional court on all
dates of hearing except when exempted specifically.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

6                                                                                       SLP(CRL.) NO.4521 OF 2026

--- Page 168 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO(S).       OF 2026
[@ SLP (C) NO.6078 OF 2025]
PALAK & ORS.                                       Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA & ORS.                            Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. IA No. 51532 of 2026 - Application for transposition is
allowed, subject to curing of defects. 
2. Leave granted.
3. The present appeal by way of special leave arises out of the
judgment and order dated 18.12.2024 passed by the High Court of
Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh in CWP No. 2946 of 2024 dismissing
the Writ Petition preferred by appellants-students.
4. The appellants before us are students who belong to the Batch
of 2019-20 pursuing B.V. Sc & AH degree programme of five and a
half year's duration at the International Institute of Veterinary
Education and Research (respondent no. 3/College), which commenced
the said course from Academic Session 2014-2015 onwards. Their fees
was initially fixed on 19.08.2015, and thereafter amended on
07.09.2017.
1

--- Page 169 ---
5.  Subsequently, the fees came to be revised by the Government
on 16.12.2020, and it was directed that the revised fee structure
would be applicable from Academic Session 2019-2020. The same was
also affirmed by the High Court in the order impugned before us
i.e., 18.12.2024.
6.  In a litigation that arose about the fixation of fees, the
Punjab and Haryana High Court passed an order on 22.01.2021
recording the statement of the advocate appearing on behalf of the
College as follows:
"Mr. Himanshu Malik, Advocate has put in
appearance  on  behalf  of  respondent  No.3-
Institute and submitted that the present writ
petition has been rendered infructuous, since
respondent No.1 vide order dated 19.01.2021 has
clarified that the fee structure, which had
been approved is for the session 2020-2021 and
not with retrospective effect. Photocopy of the
said order has also been placed on record.
Mr.  Akshay  Jindal,  Advocate  for  the
petitioners on instructions submits that since
the  students  are  of  the  batch  2019-2020,
therefore, no cause of action accrues to them
now, in view of the subsequent development. 
Accordingly, the present writ petition is
disposed  off  as  having  been  rendered
infructuous."
7. Learned  counsel  for  the  appellants  would  submit  that
determination that has taken place on 16.12.2020 by the State
Government and then by the judgment of the High Court on 18.12.2024
should not have a bearing on the fee payable for the Batch of 2019-
20 students. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent(s)
submits that the statement made by the counsel was not in the
2

--- Page 170 ---
nature of concession but was only to indicate that the Government
had passed an order on 16.12.2020 and that the remedies of the
parties were always available to challenge the said orders. 
8. Having considered the matter in detail, we are of the opinion
that the insofar as the Batch of 2019-20 is concerned,  interest of
justice will be subserved if we direct the College to return the
amount Rs. 2,50,000/- (Rupees Two lakhs and fifty thousand) to each
of the students and also not to insist payment of balance one third
of the amount payable. We order accordingly. It is also made clear
that our order will not have any bearing on the Bank Guarantees
relatable to 66% (2/3rd) which were encashed by the College. The
encashment of the  bank guarantees shall be treated as full and
final payments made by the students and there shall be no further
demand  by  the  College.  The  College  shall  not  withhold  any
certificates that they may issue to the students. 
9. We make it clear that the present order is passed in the facts
and circumstances of the case and will not have any bearing on
rights and liabilities arising out of the other academic batches. 
10. With these observations, the Civil Appeal is disposed of. 
       …………………………………………………………………………J.
   [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
     …………………………………………………………………………J.
   [ALOK ARADHE]
NEW DELHI;
 JULY 14, 2026
3

--- Page 171 ---

O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment/order dated
16.04.2026 in Criminal Miscellaneous No.22665/2026 passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Patna.
3. On  12.05.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“ 1. The petitioner challenges the
judgment and order dated 16-04-2026
in  CRM  No.22665/2026  titled
“Md.Muzaffar @ Mofiz Alam vs. State
of Bihar“ passed by the High Court
of Judicature at Patna. 
2.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
15.07.2026.
3. Mr. Manish Kumar, Advocate-on-
Record, accepts notice on behalf of
the  State  of  Bihar.  Hence,  the
formal  service  of  notice  on  the
respondent-State is waived.
1

--- Page 174 ---
4.  Learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner to serve a copy of the
present  petition  to  the  learned
counsel  for  the  respondentState
within a period of one week.
5. Let counter affidavit be filed
within two weeks. Rejoinder thereto,
if any, be filed within one week
thereafter.
6.  In  the  event  of  arrest  in
connection with F.I.R. No. 205/2025
dated 02.09.2025, registered at P.S.
Barsoi,  District  Katihar,  the
petitioner shall be released on bail
by  the  Investigating/Arresting
Officer on such terms and conditions
as imposed and found to be just,
fair and reasonable.
7. However, in the attending facts,
one  of  the  conditions  shall
necessarily  be  that  if  the
petitioner possesses a Passport, the
same shall be deposited before the
Competent Authority/Court, until the
conclusion of the Trial or unless
directed otherwise.
8. The petitioner shall make himself
available before the Investigating
Officer on 26.05.2026 at 10:00 a.m.
and on all such dates as he may be
required.
2

--- Page 175 ---
9.  Needless  to  add,  till  the
investigation  is  complete  in  all
respects, the petitioner would fully
cooperate, and if the challan/charge
sheet is filed, he would maintain
good  conduct  and  not  attempt  to
influence any of the witnesses in
any manner till the completion of
the trial. ”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 12.05.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
3

--- Page 176 ---
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment/order dated 16.04.2026 is set
aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

(SANJAY KAROL)

                   Mr. Saboor Ahmed, Adv.
                   Mr. Vemula Raghuraman, Adv.
                   Mr. Anil Kumar, AOR
                   
                   
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                      O R D E R
1. The present petition has been filed by the
petitioner against the final order and judgment
dated 10.03.2026 passed by the High Court of
Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh whereby the High
Court  has  dismissed  CRM-M-65624-2025  (O&M),
declining the prayer for anticipatory bail.
2. The  petitioner  is  apprehending  arrest  in
connection  with  FIR  No.82  of  2025  dated
10.10.2025, under Section 406 and 498-A of the
IPC.
3. We heard Ms. Shagun Shahi Chugh, learned
counsel  appearing  for  the  petitioner  and  Mr.
Siddhant Sharma, learned AoR and Mr. Sahil Kapoor,
learned counsel for the respondents.
4. We were told by the learned counsel for the
SLP (Crl.) No.8174/2026 2/5

--- Page 190 ---
party respondent that the wife and the child in
the wedlock, are in penury since the maintenance
plea before the Court in which the application has
been filed under the Protection of Women from
Domestic Violence Act, 2005 has not yet been
considered. We notice that the petitioner is a
serving officer in the Indian Army and we are
informed that the Indian Army has Regulations by
which  an  estranged  spouse  and  child,  on
application made, is provided with maintenance
directly paid from the salary, subject to any
orders issued by the competent Court.
5. Considering the status of the parties, we are
of the opinion that the plea of anticipatory bail
can  be  considered  only  if  the  petitioner  is
willing to pay maintenance to the wife and the
child. The learned counsel for the petitioner,
undertook  on  behalf  of  the  petitioner  that
maintenance would be paid, which we quantify at
Rs.20,000/- per month. However, this shall be
subject  to  any  maintenance  that  would  be
SLP (Crl.) No.8174/2026 3/5

--- Page 191 ---
determined  by  the  Army  Authorities  or  the
competent Court and whatever is paid on the basis
of  our  direction  shall  be  adjusted  from  the
arrears payable on such directions issued by the
authorities or the Court.
6. The second respondent would be entitled to
approach the Commanding Officer of the petitioner
or such other authority, praying for maintenance
which we hope would be considered expeditiously,
subject  to  whatever  quantum  is  fixed  by  the
competent Court.
7. In view of the aforesaid, we direct that in
the event of the arrest of the petitioner in
connection  with  the  Crime  number  referred  to
above, the petitioner shall be released on bail
subject to terms and conditions as may be imposed
by the Investigating Officer (I.O.).
8. It  is  needless  to  clarify  that  for  the
purpose of effective investigation the petitioner
shall cooperate with the I.O. and shall also
appear before the jurisdictional Court as and when
SLP (Crl.) No.8174/2026 4/5

--- Page 192 ---
it is so directed.
9. We make it clear that the continuance of the
bail granted by the I.O. as directed by us would
be dependent on prompt payment of the maintenance
of Rs.20,000/- per month, starting from August
2026  on  or  before  the  5 th;  continued  every
consecutive month. The petitioner shall transfer
the amount towards maintenance to the account of
the  second  respondent  by  RTGS/NEFT  mode,  to
facilitate  which  the  second  respondent  shall
provide the details of her bank account to the
petitioner.
10. With the above directions, the Special Leave
Petition is disposed of.
11. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  stand
disposed of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                   (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)               COURT MASTER (NSH)
SLP (Crl.) No.8174/2026 5/5

--- Page 194 ---
We have been appraised that the Lord Jagannath Rath
Yatra festival has commenced yesterday i.e. on 16.07.2026
and is to continue up to 27.07.2026.
The High Court by way of interim order has stated that
the appellant herein shall not release the animated film
titled “Mahaprabhu Jagannath” on 17.07.2026 and thereafter
without the leave of the Court or until the next date
whichever is earlier.
It is noted that the respondent No.7-Central Board of
Film Certification (CBFC) has permitted the exhibition of
this film and has cleared the same after considering the
film having regard to the relevant parameters.
It is stated by learned senior counsel for the
appellant that the film had earlier been exhibited as a
television  series  and  is  also  available  on  YouTube
channel. Now the very same fictional story is in the form
of an animated film and is to be exhibited in movie halls
across the country from today. 
We have also been appraised by learned Solicitor
General that from yesterday i.e. 16.07.2026 onwards for a
period  of  ten  days,  the  Lord  Jagannath  Rath  Yatra
procession and the rituals connected therewith are taking
place. In the circumstances, we permit the film to be
exhibited on or after 28.07.2026.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No. 3208/2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) No.7333/2026
ASHOK KUMAR ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH  ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard Ms. Shilpi Chowdhary, learned counsel for the
appellant  and  Mr.  Abhimanyu  Singh,  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent-State of Madhya Pradesh.
3. Appellant  seeks  anticipatory  bail  apprehending  arrest  in
connection with Crime No.538 of 2025, registered at Police Station-
Jawad, District- Neemuch, Madhya Pradesh, under Sections 8/15 and
29 of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985. The
allegation against the appellant is that, about 30 kilograms of
poppy straw was recovered from the possession of one Ramesh. In his
disclosure statement, Ramesh mentioned that the said contraband was
obtained  by  him  from  Vikas.  Vikas,  in  turn,  has  named  the
appellant.
4. Appellant’s prayer for anticipatory bail has been rejected by
1

--- Page 213 ---
the High Court vide order dated 19.01.2026.
5. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that the contraband
is well below the commercial quantity and was recovered from a
vehicle which was driven by accused Ramesh. On a query by the
Court, she submits that appellant appears to be an agricultural
laborer, though she is not very sure. She also submits that
investigation is complete and charge sheet has also been filed. In
such circumstances, appellant may be granted the benefit of pre-
arrest bail.
6. On the other hand, learned counsel for the State has referred
to the counter affidavit filed by the State and submits that
involvement of the appellant is a matter of trial and at this
stage, no conclusion can be reached  qua the involvement of the
appellant. That apart, 2 of the accused are still absconding and if
the appellant is granted anticipatory bail, it is apprehended that
appellant  may  facilitate  the  absconding  accused  from  further
evading the course of justice.
7. This Court, vide order dated 21.04.2026, had issued notice and
had granted interim protection to the appellant, subject to the
appellant cooperating with the investigation.
8. In the hearing today, We specifically asked the learned State
counsel as to whether notice has been issued by the investigating
agency to the appellant for the purpose of investigation. He fairly
submits that no such notice has been issued. However, he submits
that if the appellant is granted the benefit of pre-arrest bail at
this stage, it will hamper the arrest of the other absconding
accused.
2

--- Page 214 ---
9. From the counter affidavit, it is evident that charge sheet
has been filed by the investigating agency. In such circumstances,
and having regard to the fact that the recovery of the contraband
was from accused Ramesh and not from the appellant, we are inclined
to extend the benefit of anticipatory bail to the appellant.
10. Ordered accordingly.
11. Accordingly, the interim protection granted on 21.04.2026 is
made absolute. However, we make it clear that appellant shall
appear before the investigating agency as and when summoned. If
there is any default, it would be open to the prosecution to move
for cancellation of the anticipatory bail.
12. Consequently,  impugned  order  of  the  High  Court  dated
19.01.2026 is hereby set aside.
13. Appeal is accordingly allowed.
14. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
......................J.
(UJJAL BHUYAN)
......................J.
(ATUL S. CHANDURKAR)
NEW DELHI;
July 16, 2026.

circumstances of the present case”
