ITEM NO.33               COURT NO.10               SECTION XII-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).28212/2017
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 05-01-2017
in CMA No.1052/2005 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Hyderabad for the State of Telangana and the State of Andhra
Pradesh]
K. ANIL KUMAR BENARJI (SINCE DEAD) THR. LRS.         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
K. RATNA KUMARI                                     Respondent(s)
(IA  No.  103902/2017  -  PERMISSION  TO  FILE  ADDITIONAL
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES)
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Harsha Gollamudi, Adv.
                   Mr. Dawneesh Shaktivats, AoR
                   Ms. Kusuma, Adv.
                   Mr. Mohd Yasin, Adv.
                   Mr. Aman Sabir Saifi, Adv.
                   Ms. Himanshi Mehta, Adv.
                   Ms. Nikita Arora, Adv.
                   Mr. Kaustubh Khera, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. G. N. Reddy, AoR
                   Mr. Ankit Gaur, Adv.
                   Mr. Suraj Singh, Adv.                   
                   
          
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The present petition is directed against the impugned judgment
dated  05.01.2017  passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at

--- Page 229 ---
2
Hyderabad for the State of Telangana and for the State of Andhra
Pradesh in Civil Miscellaneous Appeal No.1052 of 2005 by which, the
decree of divorce granted by the Family Court in O.P. 766 of 2003
in favour of the petitioner/K. Anil Kumar Benarji-late husband of
the respondent-wife has been set aside.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that the Trial
Court vide its order dated 29.08.2005 held that there was cruelty
meted out by the respondent-wife and has granted decree of divorce.
It was further contended that right from the beginning, the conduct
of the respondent-wife indicated that she was not interested in
continuing the relationship as no effort was made by her to return
to the matrimonial home by filing a petition for restitution of
conjugal rights or taking any genuine steps for resumption of
matrimonial life. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that
the High Court, without considering such material which had emerged
during the proceedings before the Trial Court, has substituted its
opinion for which, there is no factual basis available on record.
It was further contended that the late husband of the respondent
had involved seniors of the family and his relatives to mediate and
ensure that the respondent returns to the matrimonial home, but
that had failed and thus, he was left with no option but to file a
petition seeking dissolution of marriage.
4. On  the  other  hand,  learned  counsel  for  the  respondent
submitted that right from the beginning, it is the case where the
respondent was forced to leave the matrimonial home in view of the
differences between the parties. It was submitted that though the
respondent was living in the matrimonial home but her husband used

--- Page 230 ---
3
to go to a village and live there for reasons, which were not
morally correct. It was contended that if at all, the husband felt
that the wife had left the matrimonial home for no valid reason,
the obvious step, which could have been taken by him, was to file a
petition for restitution of conjugal rights as he was the person
who was aggrieved by the so-called desertion of the wife, if it was
the fact.
5. According to learned counsel for the respondent, this is the
clear indication that the husband was happier and rather willing to
ensure that the wife never returned to the matrimonial home.
Learned counsel submitted that in such background, the Trial Court,
recording a finding of desertion and cruelty against the wife that
too, for having invoked her statutory right of filing complaints
before the appropriate authorities with regard to maltreatment and
harassment by the husband and the family members, cannot, by a
court of law, be held to be proof of the harassment to the husband
by the wife. It was submitted that the law provides a wife, who has
been wronged, to move before the appropriate forum created for such
redressal of her grievance and if the wife invokes the same and
exercises her statutory rights, it can in no way be construed to
mean that it is harassment.
6. In the present case, it has been found that the allegations
levelled by the wife, cannot be said to be false.
7. Learned counsel for respondent submitted that in view of the
aforesaid, from the impugned judgment, which is quite detailed, it
is clear that the case of both the sides has been dealt with in
extenso and the ultimate order passed was in accordance with law

--- Page 231 ---
4
and needs no interference.
8. We have considered the matter in its entirety and have gone
through the material on record as well as the various orders passed
by the Courts below and are convinced that the impugned judgment
cannot be said to be perverse requiring interference by this Court.
9. For reasons aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition stands
dismissed.
10. However, we clarify that this order shall not have any
prejudicial effect on the right of petitioner who is the mother of
the  deceased  husband  of  the  respondent  with  regard  to  the
petitioner’s share in law on the moveable and immovable properties
of the deceased son of the petitioner who was the husband of the
respondent. 
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 232 ---

VIKAS KUMAR                          Petitioner(s)
                        VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB                      Respondent(s)
IA No. 101305/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
WITH
SLP(Crl) No. 5683/2026 (II-B)
 
IA No. 97288/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
SLP(Crl) No. 6089/2026 (II-B)

DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 16-07-2026 These matters were called on for
hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
SLP (Crl.) No.5953/2026 1/4

--- Page 233 ---
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Muzafar Iqbal Khan, Sr.

                   Mr. Sarfraz Hamid Rather, Adv.
                   Ms. Neelam Singh, AOR
                   Ms. Neha Tiwari, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Mohd Irshad, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Karan Sharma, AOR
                   
                   
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                     O R D E R
1. An incident of eve-teasing led to a scuffle
which  escalated  into  an  altercation  involving
several persons resulting in the death of a person
and injuries to the complainant and his friend. An
FIR was lodged, by the person who accompanied the
injured who were foreign nationals. The FIR was
filed in which one of the petitioners herein,
Vikas Kumar was not named, who was roped in after
investigation. A discharge application filed was
rejected and the revision therefrom also stood
rejected. The trial court having taken cognizance
against the petitioners herein, they sought bail
which was declined.
SLP (Crl.) No.5953/2026 2/4

--- Page 234 ---
2. We heard Mr. Muzafar Iqbal Khan, learned
Senior Counsel appearing for the petitioners and
Mr. Mohd Irshad, learned AAG, appearing for the
respondent-State.
3. Admittedly there was an altercation involving
several persons. The petitioners have been in
custody for the past 10 months. The petitioners
have been roped in only because of their presence
as seen from the CCTV footage.
4. We are of the opinion that the petitioners
shall  be  released  on  bail,  subject  to  their
cooperating with the investigation.
5. The petitioners are ordered to be released on
bail, subject to terms and conditions as may be
deemed fit and proper to impose by the court of
learned Additional Sessions Judge, Kapurthala (the
jurisdictional  Court).  The  petitioners  shall
appear before the Investigating Officer (I.O.) as
and when summoned and before Court as directed by
the I.O..
6. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of.
SLP (Crl.) No.5953/2026 3/4

--- Page 235 ---
7. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  stand
disposed of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                  (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)               COURT MASTER (NSH)
SLP (Crl.) No.5953/2026 4/4

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
[Arising out of SLP(C) No.12517/2024]
HIGH COURT BAR ASSOCIATION APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS
STATE OF UTTARAKHAND & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We heard learned Counsel/Senior Counsel for the parties,
as well as the learned Solicitor General for the State
Government, and learned Additional Solicitor General for the
High Court of Uttarakhand.
3. We are satisfied that the High Court has, while passing
the  impugned  order,  exceeded  its  own  jurisdiction;
especially while suggesting the securing of the ‘opinion of
the public at large’ for the purpose of shifting of the High
Court. In our considered opinion, such like issues are not
amenable to adjudication in judicial proceedings.
4. Consequently, the appeal is allowed. The impugned order
of  the  High  Court  is  set  aside.  The  High  Court  of
Uttarakhand, on the administrative side, in consultation
with the State Government, may resolve the infrastructural
issues, if any.
5. We are informed that the State Government has earmarked
the land for construction of new building of the High Court
in District Nainital, near Haldwani. Let possession of the
1

--- Page 237 ---
said land be handed over to the High Court on ‘as is where
is’ basis within six weeks and a formal notification  re:
transfer of land be also issued within eight weeks. Ordered
accordingly.  
..........................CJI.
(SURYA KANT)
............................J.
(JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
............................J.
(V. MOHANA)

@SLP (Crl) No. 6115/2026
SARASWATI DEVI                                     Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.                  Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1.Leave granted. 
2.Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
3.This appeal arises from an order dated
28.02.2025  passed  by  the  High  Court  of
Judicature  at  Patna,  rejecting  the
anticipatory bail prayer of the appellant in
connection with Case No. 67/2017, registered
at P.S. Vigilance, District Patna.
4.After hearing the learned Counsel for the
appellant, on 02.04.2026 we had passed an
interim order, which is reproduced below:
“1. Delay condoned.
2. Issue notice, returnable in
six weeks.
1

--- Page 241 ---
3. Notice on behalf of the
respondent has been accepted
by Mr. Prem Prakash, AOR.
4.  In  the  meantime,  it  is
provided that in the event the
Petitioner  is  arrested  in
connection  with  Case  No.
67/2017,  registered  at  P.S.
Vigilance, District Patna, she
shall  be  released  on  a
personal bond of Rs. 25,000/-
subject to an undertaking that
she  shall  cooperate  in  the
investigation and make herself
available for interrogation as
and  when  required  by  the
investigating  agency.  She
shall  also  submit  an
undertaking that she will not
threaten  the  witnesses  or
tamper the evidence.”
5.Upon  service  of  notice,  the  State  is
represented.
6.Upon consideration of submissions, we do
not find a good reason to discharge the
interim protection order granted earlier.
7. This appeal is, accordingly, disposed of
by making the interim order absolute subject
2

--- Page 242 ---
to the following conditions:
(A)Within  three  weeks  from  today,  the
appellant shall submit bail bonds to the
satisfaction of the Trial Court along
with an undertaking that she shall co-
operate in the trial/investigation; and 
(B)She  shall  submit  an  additional
undertaking that she will not threaten
the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8.Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

CORRECTED
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) No.2332/2025
ARCHANA KUMARI               PETITIONER
VERSUS
SUNIL BHARTI                          RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. In  course  of  hearing  of  this  transfer  petition,  the
petitioner-wife  (Archana  Kumari)  and  the  respondent-husband
(Sunil  Bharti)  were  referred  to  the  Supreme  Court  Mediation
Centre.
2. The process of mediation has produced positive results.
The Mediator has sent a report that the parties have agreed to
resolve the inter se disputes and differences amicably. It has also
been  decided  by  them  to  apply  before  this  Court  seeking
dissolution of their marriage by mutual consent under Article 142
of the Constitution of India.
3. The parties have jointly filed an application under Article
142 of the Constitution of India, being I.A. No. 135465/2026, in
view of such settlement. The prayers in the application under
Article 142 read as follows:
“a) Allow  the  Joint  Application  by  the petitioner-wife  and
respondent-husband  in  terms  of  settlement  dated
28.04.2026 executed between the parties.
1

--- Page 253 ---
b) Exercise the power under Article 142 of the Constitution
of India and dissolve the marriage of the Petitioner and
the Respondent in light of the Settlement Agreement
Dated 28.04.2026 and to quash all the cases pending
between  the  either  of  the  parties  before  the  Courts
mentioned in para 5;
AND/OR
c) Pass  such  other  and  further  order(s)  as  this  Hon’ble
Court may deem fit and proper in the interest of justice
in the present case.”
 
4. The  respondent  instituted  Original  Suit  No.1264/2024
under section 12 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, titled “Sunil
Bharti vs. Archana Kumari”, which is pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court, Dhanbad, Jharkhand. He has agreed to have
the same quashed. The parties have also agreed to quashing of
all other litigation, currently pending by and between them in
any court/tribunal/forum.
5. The  petitioner-wife  and  the  respondent-husband  have
joined the proceedings virtually. Responding to our query, they
have separately submitted that the settlement has been arrived
at by them voluntarily and there has been no undue influence or
pressure from any side.
6. The parties having amicably resolved the inter se disputes
and differences, the terms of settlement which are contained in
the joint application shall form part of this order.
7. Accordingly, in exercise of power conferred on us by Article
142  of  the  Constitution  of  India,  we  dissolve  the  marriage
between the parties by mutual consent by granting an order in
terms of prayer (b) of the joint application. There shall also be an
2

--- Page 254 ---
order in terms of prayer (a). Decree be drawn up, accordingly.
8. As a result of this order, nothing survives for decision on
the transfer petition. The transfer petition stands disposed of. 
9. The relevant courts/police stations shall mark closure of
the  cases/proceedings/complaints  that  are  pending/lodged
before it, once any party approaches it with a copy of this order.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
11. We appreciate the efforts of the mediator in assisting the
parties to resolve their disputes amicably.
............................................J.
(DIPANKAR DATTA)
............................................J.
(SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA)

ITEM NO.21               COURT NO.5               SECTION XV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  23159/2024
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  23-02-2024
in CONT.CAS(C) No. 156/2023 passed by the High Court of Tripura at
Agarthala]
SATTYA BHUSHAN BARMAN                              Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
AJAY KUMAR BHALLA & ORS.                           Respondent(s)
Date : 15-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ALOK ARADHE
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Pranab Prakash, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Anil Kaushik, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Pranjal Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Nasadiya Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Aman Jha, Adv.
                   Mr. Rajendra Singh Rana, Adv.
                   Mr. N. Visakamurthy, Adv.
Mr. Arvind Kumar Sharma, AOR
                                      
                   Mr. Anil Kaushik, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Shreekant Neelappa Terdal, AOR
                   Mr. Pranjal Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Nasadiya Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Aman Jha, Adv.
                   Ms. Saima Bakshi, Adv.
                   
   UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present Special Leave Petition is filed against the
Judgment and order dated 23.02.2024 passed by the High Court of
Tripura dismissing the contempt petition filed by the petitioner.
2. The contempt arose in the context of a writ petition filed by
the petitioner against his non-appointment.  After hearing the

--- Page 257 ---
2
matter in detail, the High Court allowed the writ petition by its
order dated 22.08.2022 and directed as under :-
“7. It is pertinent to mention herein that
all the petitioners of this batch of writ
petitions hail from Tripura, one of the North-
Eastern states and relaxations that have been
availed by the petitioners are also availed by
all the general category candidates belonging
to the state of Tripura.
8. The subject in dispute raised in this batch
of writ petitions is squarely covered by the
judgment passed in Hemant Pokhriyal supra and
accordingly,  stands  disposed  of  with  the
directions that the respondents shall appoint
the  petitioners  against  the  remaining
vacancies  without  disturbing  the  list  of
candidates  till  date  and/or  appointments
already made by the respondents according to
the merit list of general category candidates.
9. In view of this direction, the respondents
shall  prepare  a  revised  select  list
accommodating the petitioners of this batch of
writ petitions in the merit list of general
category  of  candidates  and  appoint  them
against the available vacancies treating them

--- Page 258 ---
3
as open competition candidates. Entire process
must be completed within 2 (two) months from
today.”
3. It is evident that there is a clear and categorical direction
to appoint the petitioner.  Having not appointed, the petitioner
filed a contempt petition.
4. Mr. Anil Kaushik, learned Additional Solicitor General, has
vehemently submitted that in the counter affidavit filed in the
contempt petition, the following has been stated :-
“That the Petitioner has suppressed the above
fact that in compliance of the Order of the
Hon'ble High Court, result was revised and he
could not make it to the Select List due to
overage. The instant Contempt Petition is a
clear  abuse  of  the  process,  wherein  the
Petitioner is trying to mislead the Hon'ble
Court by filing repeatedly case on the same
facts  without  any  merit,  thereby  wasting
precious time of the Hon'ble Court.”
5. In other words, it is submitted that the petitioner could not
be appointed because he is overage in General category.
6. The High Court’s order is categorical.  There is a specific
direction to appoint the petitioner.  The State, having not
appealed against the order passed by the learned Single Judge on
22.08.2022, it has to abide by the directions given therein.  There

--- Page 259 ---
4
is no alternative to this.
7. Mr. Anil Kaushik, learned Additional Solicitor General, will
take instructions about method, manner and time with which the
petitioner will be appointed.  We will hear the learned ASG on the
next date of listing.  
8. List on 31.07.2026.
 (JAYANT KUMAR ARORA)                           (NIDHI WASON)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                       ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

--- Page 260 ---
ITEM NO.18               COURT NO.6               SECTION II-B
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No.8323/2026
[Arising  out  of  impugned  final  judgment  and  order  dated
08-07-2025 in CRMM No. 31006/2025 passed by the High Court of
Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh]
RAJINDERPAL SINGH SOHAL @ RAJINDER PAUL
SINGH & ORS.
Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB 
                                      Respondent(s)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing 
today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Ms. Tina Garg, AOR
                   Mr. M. K. Ghosh, Adv.
                                    
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Karan Sharma, AOR
                   
                   
UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The Petitioners had been denied anticipatory
bail by the High Court in connection with the First
SLP(Crl.) No.8323/2026 1

--- Page 261 ---
Information Report bearing No. 269 of 2018 registered
with  the  Police  Station  Civil  Lines  Bathinda,
District Bathinda for the offence punishable under
Sections 420/120B of  Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for
short “the IPC”) , Sections 12(1B) and 24 of the
Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992 (for
short “the SEBI Act”) read with Regulation 3 of SEBI
(Collective Investment Scheme) Regulation Act, 1999,
and 4(2)(1) of the SEBI (Prohibition of Fraudulent
and Unfair Trade Practice Relating to Securities
market) Regulations, 2003.
2. While issuing notice, we had ordered that no
coercive steps be taken against the Petitioners who
are aged 70, 86, and 52 years respectively.
3. We are informed that the 1st Petitioner is no
more and hence the SLP against him stands abated.
4. The Petitioners were initially booked for the
offences under Sections 420 and 120B of IPC in FIR
269 of 2018; the provisions under the SEBI Act and
the  associated  Regulations  were  added  after  the
completion of investigation. The Punjab and Haryana
SLP(Crl.) No.8323/2026 2

--- Page 262 ---
High Court had initially granted interim anticipatory
bail to the Petitioners – prior to the addition of
the  charges  under  the  SEBI  Act  and  associated
regulations – and the interim anticipatory bail had
later been made absolute.
5. However, after the addition of the charges under
the SEBI Act and the associated Regulations, the
Petitioners approached the Sessions Judge, Bathinda
for anticipatory bail for the enhanced offences,
which was dismissed through order dated 15.05.2025 in
BA/1656/2025.  Subsequently,  the  Petitioners
approached the Punjab and Haryana High Court seeking
the same relief, which was rejected through order
dated 08.07.2025 in CRM-M-31006-2025. 
6. We  heard  Ms.Tina  Garg,  the  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner and Mr. Karan Sharma,
the learned counsel appearing for the respondent. 
7. The foundational facts on which the FIR was
first registered under Sections 420 and 120B of IPC
remain the same, when the provisions of the SEBI Act
were later included. This was also on the basis of a
SLP(Crl.) No.8323/2026 3

--- Page 263 ---
communication  received  from  the  SEBI  requesting
necessary action against the Petitioners who were
directors of a Company which engaged in mobilization
of funds with promise of returns without obtaining a
certificate of registration from SEBI. Since in the
earlier FIR the Petitioners were granted Anticipatory
Bail, we order that in the event of arrest of the
Petitioners  in  connection  with  the  offences
enumerated above, they shall be released on bail,
subject  to  the  terms  and  conditions  that  the
Investigating Officer may deem fit to impose.
8. We make it clear that the Petitioner Nos.2 and 3
who are granted bail by the Investigating Officer
(“IO”), shall cooperate with the investigation and
shall appear before the jurisdictional court when so
directed  by  the  I.O.  With  the  aforesaid,  this
Petition stands disposed of.
9. Pending  applications,  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.
   (CHANDRESH)                                (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                      COURT MASTER (NSH)
SLP(Crl.) No.8323/2026 4

Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No.10632/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 08-04-2026
in CRMM No.13718/2026 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana

BALBIR KAUR @BEERO                                 Petitioner(s)

WITH
SLP(Crl) No. 10633/2026 (II-B)
 FOR PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES ON IA 
170393/2026 
FOR EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T. ON IA 170395/2026
IA No. 170395/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 170393/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL

 FOR PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES ON IA

FOR EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T. ON IA 169761/2026
IA No. 169761/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 169759/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 16-07-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
SLP(Crl.) No.10632/2026 1

--- Page 265 ---
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Ms. Meenakshi Arora, Sr. Adv.
                   Mrs. Tanuj Bagga Sharma, AOR
                   Dr. M.K Ravi, Adv.
                   Mr. Denson Joseph, Adv.
                   Mr. Navdeep Singh, Adv.
                                      
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Karan Sharma, AOR
                                
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The accused in FIR registered under Sections 80 and 61 of
the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (for short “the BNS Act
2023”) are before us, challenging the separate orders passed
rejecting Anticipatory Bail to two of the accused and Regular
Bail to another accused. The above Special Leave Petitions are
filed by the son, Inderjit Singh, (SLP (Crl.) No. 10633 of
2026), father Kuldeep Singh (SLP (Crl.) No. 10548 of 2026),
and mother Balbir Kaur (SLP (Crl.) No. 10632 of 2026), in the
case of suicidal death of the wife of the son.
 
2. We heard Ms. Meenakshi Arora, learned Senior Counsel for
the Petitioners, and Mr. Karan Sharma, AOR for the State of
Punjab. 
SLP(Crl.) No.10632/2026 2

--- Page 266 ---
3. The learned Senior Counsel pointed out that the parents-
in-law have been included merely to harass them and the
husband was not even accused of any complicity in the suicide.
It is also pointed out that the family of the deceased has a
history of suicidal tendencies and she was suffering from
depression. The learned counsel for the State however points
out that there was an audio clip of the deceased clearly
blaming the mother-in-law and the sisters-in-law, alleging
that their conduct prompted the deceased to carry out the
drastic act of taking one’s own life. It was also revealed on
post-mortem that the deceased was pregnant at the time of her
death. 
4. Considering the facts and circumstances and the severity
of the crime, we are not inclined to grant anticipatory bail
to the husband of the deceased, and SLP (Crl.) No. 10633/2026
stands dismissed. The husband of the deceased is granted a
week’s time to surrender before the Investigating Officer
(I.O.) who shall take appropriate steps, under the Bharatiya
Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (for short “the BNSS Act,
2023”).
5. Insofar as the father is concerned (SLP (Crl.) No. 10548
SLP(Crl.) No.10632/2026 3

--- Page 267 ---
of 2026), he has been in jail for more than a year. In such
circumstances, he shall be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the Sessions Judge (Fast Track Court) Amritsar
deem fit and proper to impose. The Special Leave Petition is
disposed of.
6. Insofar as the mother-in-law is concerned, SLP (Crl.) No.
10632 of 2026) is allowed, directing the I.O. to grant
anticipatory bail to the petitioner on her appearance, within
a period of two weeks from today, on such terms and conditions
as may be fixed by the I.O. The SLP stands allowed.
7. It is made clear that the accused who are allowed to be
released on bail and anticipatory bail shall cooperate with
the investigation and appear before the I.O. as and when
summoned. They shall also appear before the jurisdictional
court as directed by the I.O.
8. The Special Leave Petitions are ordered accordingly. 
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
   (CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)
   ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
SLP(Crl.) No.10632/2026 4

2023”) are before us, challenging the separate orders passed
rejecting Anticipatory Bail to two of the accused and Regular
Bail to another accused. The above Special Leave Petitions are
filed by the son, Inderjit Singh, (SLP (Crl.) No. 10633 of
2026), father Kuldeep Singh (SLP (Crl.) No. 10548 of 2026),
and mother Balbir Kaur (SLP (Crl.) No. 10632 of 2026), in the
case of suicidal death of the wife of the son.
 
2. We heard Ms. Meenakshi Arora, learned Senior Counsel for
the Petitioners, and Mr. Karan Sharma, AOR for the State of
Punjab. 
SLP(Crl.) No.10632/2026 2

--- Page 282 ---
3. The learned Senior Counsel pointed out that the parents-
in-law have been included merely to harass them and the
husband was not even accused of any complicity in the suicide.
It is also pointed out that the family of the deceased has a
history of suicidal tendencies and she was suffering from
depression. The learned counsel for the State however points
out that there was an audio clip of the deceased clearly
blaming the mother-in-law and the sisters-in-law, alleging
that their conduct prompted the deceased to carry out the
drastic act of taking one’s own life. It was also revealed on
post-mortem that the deceased was pregnant at the time of her
death. 
4. Considering the facts and circumstances and the severity
of the crime, we are not inclined to grant anticipatory bail
to the husband of the deceased, and SLP (Crl.) No. 10633/2026
stands dismissed. The husband of the deceased is granted a
week’s time to surrender before the Investigating Officer
(I.O.) who shall take appropriate steps, under the Bharatiya
Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (for short “the BNSS Act,
2023”).
5. Insofar as the father is concerned (SLP (Crl.) No. 10548
SLP(Crl.) No.10632/2026 3

--- Page 283 ---
of 2026), he has been in jail for more than a year. In such
circumstances, he shall be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the Sessions Judge (Fast Track Court) Amritsar
deem fit and proper to impose. The Special Leave Petition is
disposed of.
6. Insofar as the mother-in-law is concerned, SLP (Crl.) No.
10632 of 2026) is allowed, directing the I.O. to grant
anticipatory bail to the petitioner on her appearance, within
a period of two weeks from today, on such terms and conditions
as may be fixed by the I.O. The SLP stands allowed.
7. It is made clear that the accused who are allowed to be
released on bail and anticipatory bail shall cooperate with
the investigation and appear before the I.O. as and when
summoned. They shall also appear before the jurisdictional
court as directed by the I.O.
8. The Special Leave Petitions are ordered accordingly. 
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
   (CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  3196/2026
SLP (Crl.) No(s). 6572/2026
KAMAL TONDE                                        Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF CHHATTISGARH                          Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard  learned  counsel  for  the
parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order
dated 13.02.2026 passed by the High Court of
Chhattisgarh  rejecting  the  regular  bail
prayer of the appellant in connection with
Crime  No.  360/2025,  registered  at  P.S.
Simga,  District  Baloda-Bazar  Bhatapara
(C.G.).
4. The submission of the learned counsel
for the appellant is that appellant is not
named  in  the  First  Information  Report
1

--- Page 285 ---
(“FIR”). There were as many as 16 accused
out of which 9 have been admitted to bail.
The only distinguishing feature in the case
of the appellant with the other accused is
that he has been identified in the Test
Identification  Parade  (T.I.P).  It  is
submitted that the credibility of the T.I.P
would be tested in the trial but, for the
time being, it may be noticed that the T.I.P
was conducted more than two months after the
arrest. It has also been submitted that the
appellant  has  no  previous  criminal
antecedents. 
5. The learned Counsel for the State-
respondent has opposed the prayer for bail.
6. Having regard to the fact that 9 out
of 16 accused have been admitted to bail and
the  appellant  has  no  previous  criminal
antecedents  and  has  already  suffered
incarceration for a period of over one year,
we are of the view that the appellant is
entitled to be released on bail pending
trial. 
2

--- Page 286 ---
7. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed.
The order passed by the High Court rejecting
the regular bail prayer of the appellant is
set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on
bail on such terms and conditions as the
trial court may deem fit to impose in the
facts and circumstances of the case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.

Civil Appeal No(s). 8873/2011
1. The Civil Appeal is dismissed in terms of the  
signed order placed on file.
2

--- Page 290 ---
2. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand  
disposed of.
Special Leave Petition(Civil) No. 18831/2011
1. It  is  stated  that  along  with  Civil  Appeal  
No.21847/2017, this matter had already been dismissed  
on the ground of low tax effect. However, from the ROPs 
of this proceeding, we do not find the said order being 
reflected.
2. As such, we reiterate that on account of low tax  
effect, the Special Leave Petition is dismissed.
3. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand  
disposed of.
Special Leave Petition(Civil) No. 35552/2013
1. The issue involved in this Special Leave Petition  
is no more res integra.
2. In view of the order of this Court referred in  
Civil Appeal No.9008 of 2011, wherein it came to be  
held as under:
“On the facts of these cases we find that the  
assessee had been rightly held entitled to  
reversal of the Cenvat Credit in respect of  
the inputs which were produced with effect  
from  01.08.2004  the  date  from  which  the  
assessee was granted exemption from payment  
of excise duty in respect of final products.  
3

--- Page 291 ---
Thus, when the reverse of Cenvat Credit was  
given to the assessee on that date, final  
product was not exempted. In view thereof,  
Rule  6(1)  of  the  Cenvat  Credit  Rules,  
2002/2004  shall  not  be  applied  since  the 
present cases are squarely covered by the  
judgment  dated  11.08.1999  passed  by  this  
Court in Collector of Central Excise, Pune  
vs. Dai Ichi Karkaria Ltd. [1999 (112) E.L.T.  
353 S.C.)].”
3. The present Special Leave Petition fails and is,  
accordingly, dismissed.
4. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand  
disposed of.
(ABHINAV KUMAR)                                 (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

                           O R D E R
 SLP (Crl.) No. 7886/2026
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The petitioner is accuse d in Crime No. 117/2024 registered at  
Police Station - GRP, Bilaspur, District- Bilaspur (C.G.), for the  
offences punishable under Sections 20(B)and 29 of the Narcotic  
Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985.
3. The allegation against the petitioner is that he was caught 
with a bag in which there was 10 kg of Ganja.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that the same was  
not recovered from the conscious possession of the petitioner and  
that he has been in custody for almost one year and nine months. It  
was further contendend that there is a lot of discrepancy with  
regard to the search and seizure procedure which clearly goes  
against  the  prosecution  and,  being  mandatory  provisions,  the  
prosecution case has to fail. Learned counsel for the petitioner  
submitted that the petitioner is in jail in connection with the  
present case and he was granted bail in the other two cases.
5. Learned counsel for the State submitted that the petitioner  
has two other antecedents, both under the NDPS Act.
6. Having considered the matter, we find that, for the present,  
the petitioner has not made out a case for grant of bail.
7. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed
8. Pending applications, if any, shall stand disposed of.

--- Page 294 ---
SLP(Crl) No. 7898/2026 
Heard learned Senior counsel for the petitioner and learned  
counsel for the State.
2. The petitioner is accuse d in Crime No. 117/2024 registered at  
Police Station - GRP, Bilaspur, District- Bilaspur (C.G.), for the  
offences punishable under Sections 20(B)and 29 of the Narcotic  
Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985.
3. Learned Senior  counsel for the petitioner submits that the  
petitioner is not named in the FIR and only on the statement of a  
co-accused he has been made an accused. It was further submitted  
that the petitioner is a constable in the GRP and just because it  
was stated by the co-accused that they used to carry out the  
business  in  connivance  with  the  GRP  constables,  i.e.,  the  
petitioner and others, he cannot be penalized, at least in the  
present case, unless it is proved in accordance with law during  
trial.
4. It was further submitted that the petitioner is in custody for  
about one year and seven months.
5. Learned counsel for the State submitted that the petitioner  
being a GRP constable was indulging in such activity which has been  
stated by the co-accused. It has been further submitted that the  
petitioner along with three other GRP constables has also been  
placed under suspension and has been proceeded against. It was  
submitted that there was no occasion for false implication of the  
petitioner being a GRP constable.
6. Having considered the matter in its entirety and going through  

--- Page 295 ---
the material on record, we are not persuaded to allow the prayer  
for bail to the petitioner, for the present.
7. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
8. Pending applications, if any, shall stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.35               COURT NO.10               SECTION XI-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  16594/2023
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 27-04-2023  
in WP(C) No. 12788/2023 passed by the High Court of Orissa at  
Cuttack]
BIMAL PRASAD PATTNAIK                              Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                              Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 149361/2023 - PERMISSION TO FILE LENGTHY LIST OF DATES
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANMOHAN 
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Amit Pai, AoR
                   Mr. Sashwat Pattnaik, Adv.
                   Ms. Pankhuri Bhardwaj, Adv.
  
  
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Vikramjeet Banerjee, A.S.G.
                   Ms. Sakshi Kakkar, Adv.
                   Mr. Prashant Singh-i, Adv.
                   Ms. Vishakha, Adv.
                   Ms. Priyanka Das, Adv.
                   Mr. Manish, Adv.
                   Mr. Harish Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Amrish Kumar,AoR
         Ms. Sanjana, Adv.
                   Mr. Sudarshan Lamba, AoR
  
  
                            O R D E R
Heard Mr. Amit Pai, learned counsel for the petitioner and Mr.  
Vikramjeet Banerjee, learned ASG for the respondents.
2. The petitioner seeks payment of interest on the so-called  
delayed payment of his retirement dues and also for payment of  

--- Page 297 ---
amount unpaid under various heads.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that the order of  
this Court dated 22.08.2017 passed in C.A. No. 10935 of 2017, by  
which the order of dismissal of the petitioner from service was  
converted into that of compulsory retirement by this Court, was  
required to be complied within four months and the respondents were 
required to release all the consequential dues. However, according  
to him, the consequential dues would mean whatever was due on the  
day the Court passed the order. It is his contention that since  
2017, more than five years had elapsed from the date on which the  
petitioner would have otherwise superannuated, and that any delayed  
payment would ipso facto entail payment of interest also for the  
loss suffered by the petitioner on the amount not paid to him and  
which was due to him. It has been further submitted that this Court  
in the case of D.D. Tewari (D) Thr. Lrs. v. Uttar Haryana Bijli  
Vitran Nigam Ltd. & Ors., (2014) 8 SCC 894,  had categorically held 
that  whatever  amount  which  is  due  to  an  employee  who  has  
superannuated and not paid to him/her on time would carry interest  
on it.
4. Learned ASG submitted that within four months of the order of  
this Court, the dues of the petitioner have been paid with  
interest, if there has been any delay on such payment. However, he  
contended that the order cannot be read to mean that interest was  
included in the direction of the Court in the term consequential  
benefits which has to be read together with the word “as was due to  
him". It was submitted that the petitioner was only entitled to all  
dues as admissible under the Rules, which have been paid and the  
Rules did not include or even contemplate any payment of interest.  

--- Page 298 ---
It was submitted that interest is a separate component and unless  
the Court independently applies its mind and specifically grants  
the same, it cannot be read into the order of payment of the  
admissible dues to any retired person. It was further contended  
that a chart has been given with regard to the payments made which  
is not in dispute. However, with regard to three heads under which  
payment is claimed and is said to have not been made, it was the  
stand of Mr. Vikramjeet Banerjee, learned ASG that the petitioner  
is not entitled to be paid as such benefits were benefits given to  
a person either in service and upon superannuation, but would lapse  
thereafter  and  further,  the  same  were  basically  by  way  of  
reimbursement on actuals.
5. However, with regard to arrears of DA, it was submitted that  
the same has been paid and the amount which has been indicated in  
the affidavit has not been rebutted. 
6. We have given our anxious thoughts to the entire matter and  
also taking note of the judgment in the case of D.D. Tewari (D)  
Thr.  Lrs.  (supra),  relied  upon  by  learned  counsel  for  the  
petitioner, we do not find any error in the order impugned. The  
payments  made  to  the  petitioner  by  the  respondents,  in  our  
considered  opinion,  satisfies  the  order  of  this  Court  dated  
22.08.2017 in C.A. No. 10935/2017. We also indicate here that the  
petitioner is raising a claim based on the interpretation of the  
order of this Court dated 22.08.2017 in C.A. No. 10935/2017,  
whereas if at all he had to agitate the matter, it could only have  
been by seeking a clarification or modification of the order of  
this Court to include payment of interest to him for the period  
when he would have retired till passing of the order, which has not  

--- Page 299 ---
been done. 
7. For reasons aforesaid, we find no merit in the present  
petition and accordingly the same stands dismissed. 
8. Pending applications, if any, stand disposed of.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)                         ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

--- Page 300 ---
Part-heard RoP-1

representing the State of West Bengal, states that the State does

accordingly, dismissed as withdrawn.

well as Mr. Kunal Chatterji, learned counsel, appearing for  the
West  Bengal  Commission  for  Backward  Class , SLP(C)  Diary
No.45526/2025, SLP(C) No.26497/2025 & SLP(C) Diary No.36073/2024,
are also taken on Board. As prayed for, the same are dismissed as
withdrawn.
3. The withdrawal of the aforesaid matters shall not preclude any
other affected person from approaching this Court in accordance
with law.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  7899/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 31-03-2026
in MCRLC No. 9214/2026 passed by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh
Principal Seat at Jabalpur]
DEEPAK KUMAR RAY                                   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH                        Respondent(s)
(IA No. 132174/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT)
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Prathvi Raj Chauhan, AOR
                   Mr. Venkatesh Rajput, Adv.
                   Mr. Hanumant Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Vipul Kumar, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Sarthak Raizada- G.a., Adv.
                   Ms. Mrinal Gopal Elker, AOR
                   Mr. Hanmat Singh Lodhi, Adv.
                   Ms. Silpi S Swain, Adv.                         
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The  present  Special  Leave  Petition  calls  in
SLP (Crl.) No.7899/2026 Page 1 of 3

--- Page 309 ---
 
question the correctness of the order dated 31 st March
2026  passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Madhya  Pradesh,
Principal Seat at Jabalpur in Misc.Criminal Case No.9214
of 2026. By the said order, the petitioner has been
denied the benefit of regular bail in connection with
FIR/Crime No.377 of 2023 registered at Police Station –
Madan Mahal, District Jabalpur for offences punishable
under Section 420, 406, 34 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860
and Section 66 of the IT Act.
2. Heard  Mr.  Prathvi  Raj  Chauhan,  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner and Mr. Sarthak Raizada,
learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State.
3. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  petitioner
submits that the petitioner has already undergone 1 year
and 11 months in custody.  Learned counsel also submits
that the case is of civil nature.
4. Learned counsel for the respondent-State vehemently
opposes the contention of the petitioner and submits that
the petitioner has received Rs.11,80,000/- (Rupees eleven
lakhs eighty thousand) in his bank account.
5. Having  considered  the  period  of  incarceration
already undergone and the fact that the trial is likely
to take some time,  we  direct that pending trial, the
SLP (Crl.) No.7899/2026 Page 2 of 3

--- Page 310 ---
 
petitioner shall be enlarged on bail subject to the
satisfaction of the Trial Court.  
6. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
7. Pending application shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)
     DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          COURT MASTER
SLP (Crl.) No.7899/2026 Page 3 of 3

--- Page 311 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL)  NO.3336/2025
KRITIKA                                       Petitioner(s)
                         VERSUS
MAYANK DANG                                   Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Through this petition, the petitioner – wife seeks
transfer  of  HMA  No.251  of  2025  filed  by  the
respondent – husband in the Family Court North-West
District, Rohini, New Delhi. 
 
2. In ground (d) of the petition, the petitioner made
a statement that she is not working anywhere and
has no source of income.
3. In the counter affidavit, it has been stated that
the petitioner is working as a Senior SOC Design
Engineer with Intel Corporation, Inc (USA) and
drawing a handsome salary.  It has also been stated
that the petitioner is a frequent traveler and has
also participated in a Beauty pageant held in
Manila (Philippines) in July, 2023.  In support of
1

--- Page 312 ---
the  aforesaid  statement,  documents  have  been
annexed.
4. The aforesaid stand could not be refuted.
5. Having regard to the above, we decline to entertain
the transfer request. The transfer petition is,
accordingly, dismissed.  However, it shall be open
to the petitioner to participate in the proceedings
through video conferencing mode subject to the
orders to the contrary by the court concerned.  If
the court denies the facility of video conferencing
on the application submitted by the petitioner, it
shall record reasons for doing so.
6. All pending applications shall stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………J
                [MANOJ MISRA]
…………………………………………………J
New Delhi                              [VIJAY BISHNOI]
July 15, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
ORIGINAL CIVIL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL)  NO.385/2026
KALAVALA VENKATA NAGA LAKSHMI BHARATHI        Petitioner(s)
                           VERSUS
SRIDHARA SURENDRA BABU                        Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The  petitioner  –  wife  seeks  transfer  of  divorce
proceedings bearing F.C.O.P. No.19/2023 pending in the
Family Court, Kadapa, Andhra Pradesh to the Family Court,
Hyderabad, Telangana.
3. In the counter affidavit, a stand has been taken that
the subject proceedings are nearing completion inasmuch as
only cross-examination of the petitioner remains before
final arguments.  It has also been submitted on behalf of
the respondent that the petitioner had participated in the
proceedings for as long as three years and only when the
proceedings were nearing completion, the transfer petition
was filed.
4. The learned counsel for the respondent could not
1

--- Page 315 ---
dispute that the proceedings are nearing completion and
only petitioner’s cross-examination remains before final
arguments.  In such circumstances, we decline the prayer to
transfer the proceedings.  The petition is, accordingly,
dismissed.
5. It is, however, made clear that the petitioner would
have liberty to participate in the proceedings through
video conferencing if her physical presence is not required
under  orders  of  the  Court  where  the  proceedings  are
pending. It is also made clear that if a request is made by
the petitioner seeking exemption from physical appearance,
the same shall be accorded due consideration and if the
Court declines the same, it shall record reasons.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………J
                 [MANOJ MISRA]
…………………………………………………J
New Delhi                              [VIJAY BISHNOI]
July 15, 2026
2

IA No. 193517/2026 - STAY APPLICATION
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KAROL
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Anil Kumar, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present Transfer Petition has been filed seeking
transfer of proceedings being family case  vide Petition
No.A-1208/2025, titled “ Mr. Malikarjun Dhangar vs. Mrs.
Jyothi Malikarjun Dhangar Maiden Name Jyothi Karva”, on the
file of the Family Court Judge, Bandra, Mumbai, Maharashtra
to the Principal Family Court having competent jurisdiction
at Hyderabad, Telangana.
2. On the first call, the matter was taken up. None has
entered appearance on behalf of the petitioner. Hence the
matter was passed over. Even on the second call, after the
pass over, when none appeared, this Court, in the interest
1

--- Page 336 ---
of justice, requested Ms. Visakhonvo Natso, (Email ID:
visakhonvonatso@gmail.com and Enrolment No. D/2626/2026), a
young counsel present in the Court to assist the Court as
an amicus curiae (pro bono). To enable the learned amicus
to read the Court file and assist the Court, the matter was
again passed over. Even on the third call, when none
appeared  for  the  petitioner,  the  learned  amicus was
permitted to assist the Court. 
3. Heard the submissions advanced by the learned amicus
curiae.
4. Issue notice, returnable on 27.08.2026.
5. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted. Let steps for
service be taken within two (02) weeks.
6. In the notice itself, let it be mentioned that the
respondent is required to file the counter affidavit and
reply to the interlocutory application(s), if any, before
the next date of listing.
7. In the meanwhile, all further proceedings inter se the
parties, arising out of matrimonial discord, shall remain
stayed. An endeavour shall be made to have all disputes
arising out of matrimonial discord, whether pending before
different forums or under contemplation, settled amicably.
8. However, if there is any order of maintenance passed by
any appropriate authority, the same shall continue to be
complied with by the respondent-husband.
9.  It is clarified that the learned amicus curiae shall
continue to assist the Court as such.
2

--- Page 337 ---
10.  The Registry shall print the name of the learned
amicus curiae in the cause list.
11. The  Registry  is  directed  to  supply  the  complete
paperbook, in digital form to the learned  amicus curiae
within a period of three working days.

IA No. 194955/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KAROL
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. T. Mahipal, AOR
                   Mr. Surya Kamal Mishra, Adv.
                   Mr. Rishabh Thakur, Adv. 
                  
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner no.1/husband seeks transfer of petition
bearing Criminal Misc. Application No.3911 of 2025, titled
“Hirva Abhishek Bijwaria (Saini) Vs. Abhishek Sureshbhai @
Sureshkumar Bijwaria (Saini) & Ors. ”, pending before the Court
of learned Chief Judicial Magistrate, Rajkot, Gujarat, to the
Court of learned Judicial Magistrate, Chandigarh, Punjab.
2. It is submitted that petitioner no.1/husband and the
respondent/wife are both residing in Qatar. The respondent has
1

--- Page 339 ---
initiated proceedings falsely implicating not only her husband,
but also her in-laws, who are petitioner nos.2 and 3 (father-
in-law and mother-in-law respectively).
3. It is further submitted that post-receipt of the summons
in  the  proceedings  initiated  by  the  respondent-wife,  the
petitioner no.2 had suffered heart attack. On account of
ailment, both petitioner nos.2 and 3 are not in a condition to
travel to Rajkot, Gujarat, as they are residing at Chandigarh.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioners further submits that
petitioner no.1/husband is ready and willing to settle all the
issues arising out of the matrimonial discord  inter se  the
parties. 
5. Since the dispute primarily arises out of matrimonial
discord, this Court would implore upon the parties to endeavour
an amicable resolution of the dispute, which would not only
ensure finality and certainty of their relationship but also
the several proceedings which are pending inter se the parties.
6. Issue notice, returnable on 07.09.2026.
7. Dasti service, in addition, is permitted. Let steps for
service in all respects, be positively taken within two weeks.
8. In the notice itself, let it be mentioned that respondent
is  required  to  file  counter  affidavit  and  reply  to  the
interlocutory application(s), if any, before the next date of
listing.
9. In the meanwhile, all further proceedings  inter se  the
parties, arising out of matrimonial discord, shall remain
stayed.
2

--- Page 340 ---
10. However, if there is any order of maintenance passed by
any appropriate authority, the same shall continue to be
complied with by the petitioner no.1/husband.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 3487/2025
SAMREEN ..... PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
MUSTAJAB HUSSAIN ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
Having heard the learned counsel for the parties and having
perused the averments made in the transfer petition, we allow the
transfer petition in the interest of justice.
Accordingly, Matrimonial Case No. 1219/2025, titled “Mustajab
Husain vs. Samreen ”, pending before the learned Principal Judge,
Family Court, Muzaffarnagar, Uttar Pradesh, is directed to be
transferred  to  the  learned   Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,
Karkardooma Courts, District-Shahdara, Delhi, who may hear and
dispose of the case or transfer it to another Court of competent
jurisdiction.
Records of the case shall be immediately transferred to the
transferee Court.
To cut short delay, parties are directed to appear before the
transferee Court on 03.08.2026. On the said date and thereafter,
the parties may appear in person or through their authorized
representatives or through video-conferencing, if the said facility
1

--- Page 348 ---
is available in the transferee Co urt and is permitted by it. In
case there is non-appearance on behalf of any party on the date
fixed by this Court, notice shall be issued by the transferee Court
to the defaulting party.
The parties may explore the possibility of settlement through
mediation. The transferee Court may obtain the desire and wishes of
the parties in this regard and proceed accordingly.
The transferee Court may decide the matter expeditiously.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
.....................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
.....................J.
(SANJEEV SACHDEVA)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026.

ITEM NO.26               COURT NO.14               SECTION XII-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No(s). 40871/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  10-07-2026 
in WA No. 832/2026 passed by the High Court of Andhra Pradesh at 
Amravati]
M/S. CORPUS ENTERPRISES PRIVATE LIMITED            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF ANDHRA PRADESH & ANR.                 Respondent(s)
IA No. 196594/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 196593/2026 - PERMISSION TO FILE SLP WITHOUT CERTIFIED/PLAIN 
COPY OF IMPUGNED ORDER
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. C Kranthi Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Danish Saifi, Adv.
                   Mr. Shafik Ahmed, Adv.
                   Ms. Abida, Adv.
                   Mr. S Subramaniam, Adv.
                   Mr. Vv Kk Hanumanth Prasad, Adv.
                   Mr. Abdul Gaffar, Adv.
                   Mr. Rashid N. Azam, AOR
  
  
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Byrapaneni Suyodhan, Adv.
                   Ms. Obulapuram Keerthi, Adv.
                   Ms. Esha Thawal, Adv.
                   Ms. Tatini Basu, AOR
  
  
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. C. Kranthi Kumar, learned counsel for the petitioner  

--- Page 363 ---
and Mr. Byrapaneni Suyodhan, lerned counsel for the respondent No.2
2. The present Special Leave Petition calls in question the  
correctness of the order dated 10.07.2026 in Writ Appeal No. 832 of  
2026. The said writ petition was filed assailing the order dated  
30.06.2026, passed in I.A No. 1 of 2026 in Writ Petition No. 15339  
of 2026.
3. By the said order, the learned Single Judge declined to grant 
an interim order of stay of the proceedings arising out of the  
impugned Request for Proposal(RFP) bearing -Ref. No.-APSFL/MW-DRM  
SW/2026 dated 21.05.2026 issued by the Respondent No.2/APSFL, and  
further refused the prayer of the petitioner for extension of time  
for submission of its bid/proposal  and made the process subject to  
the result of the writ petition. 
4. We are not setting out the facts in detail, lest it prejudice  
either parties at the final hearing of this Writ Petition. The  
petitioner's grievance is that the tender is issued when his  
contract for the same work is subsisting and also that in the new  
RFP, a clause has been inserted, seeking a declaration that the  
bidders have no show cause notices issued against them.
5. According to the petitioner, with regard to the execution of  
work with the respondent No.2, there are two show cause notices and  
in the teeth of those show cause notices, he is not able to give  
the declaration and as such he is not able to participate in the  
bid. It is also his case that the tender itself was for a project  
which the petitioner is in the process of executing on account of  
an award given to him earlier.
6. Mr. Suyodhan, learned counsel for the State disputes each of  

--- Page 364 ---
the said contentions. According to him, the petitioner was at fault  
and did not carry out the work entrusted to him and considering the  
nature of the work, the Authority cannot be foreclosed from issuing  
fresh tender. These and other matters have to be adjudicated in the  
main Writ Petition.
7. The main Writ Petition is posted on 21.07.2026. We are of  
opinion that till the main Writ Petition is heard and decided, no  
further steps should be taken in the request for proposal (RFP)  
read with the corrigendum which has been impugned in the present  
Writ Petition. The High Court shall make an endeavour to hear the  
Writ Petition itself on 21.07.2026 or on such earlier date that the  
High Court may deem fit to notify the matter.
8. With the aforesaid observations, this Special Leave Petition  
is disposed of.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (MANOJ KUMAR)
  COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 365 ---
1

ITEM NO.12               COURT NO.15               SECTION II-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  11762/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 09-01-2026
in IA No. 6979/2025 passed by the High Court of Jharkhand at
Ranchi]
SHAMBHU MEHTA ALIAS SHAMBHU PRASAD MEHTA           Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND                             Respondent(s)
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This petition was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s): Mr. Subhro Sanyal, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s): Mr. Jayant Mohan, AOR
    Mr. Dhawal Mohan, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner
and  learned  counsel  for  the  respondent-State  of
Jharkhand.
2. Petitioner  is  aggrieved  by  order  dated
07/09.01.2026 passed by the High Court of Jharkhand
(briefly the ‘High Court’ hereinafter), rejecting I.A.
No.6979 of 2025 filed by the petitioner for suspension
1

--- Page 372 ---
of sentence and for releasing him on bail during
pendency of the Criminal Appeal filed by him before the
High Court, being Cr. Appeal (S.J.) No.495 of 2025.
3. It is stated that petitioner was convicted by the
Sessions Judge, Hazaribagh in Sessions Trial No.43 of
2020 under Section 304 Part-II of the Indian Penal
Code, 1860 (in short ‘IPC’) and sentenced to undergo
Rigorous Imprisonment (R.I.) for a period of five
years.
4. Aggrieved  by  the  aforesaid  conviction  and
sentence, petitioner has preferred the related Criminal
Appeal before the High Court. Petitioner also preferred
the aforesaid Interlocutory Application for suspension
of sentence. However, by way of the impugned order, the
prayer for suspension of sentence has been rejected.
5. This Court vide order dated 24.06.2026 had issued
notice, making the case returnable today.
6. Though learned counsel for the respondent-State of
Jharkhand prays for time to file counter affidavit, we
are not inclined to accede to the request so made for
the reason that petitioner has already undergone more
than 01 year 05 months of incarceration out of the
sentence of five years. That apart, it is a case where
one of the labourers working in the mine of the
petitioner was found dead in the mine.
7. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that
2

--- Page 373 ---
merely because the villagers stated that the mine
belonged to the petitioner, cannot be a good enough
reason  for  conviction  of  the  petitioner  for  the
homicidal death of the deceased. In fact, he submits
that the mine from where the dead body was recovered
does not belong to the petitioner.
8. Be that as it may, without expressing any opinion
on merit, we allow the prayer of the petitioner by
directing that the petitioner shall be produced before
the concerned Sessions Court within a maximum period of
07 days from today, whereafter the Sessions Court shall
release the petitioner on bail on such terms and
conditions as may be deemed appropriate.
9. Accordingly,  the  Special  Leave  Petition  is
disposed of.
10. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
(ABHINAV KUMAR)                                (CHETNA BALOONI)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 374 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION(S) (CIVIL) NO.   2922/2025  
MANISHA RATNAKAR                                    Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
KUMAR ABHISHEK                                      Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. The present petition has been filed for transfer of
matrimonial dispute i.e. H.M.A. No. 610 of 2025, titled as
“Kumar Abhishek vs. Manisha Ratnakar” , pending before the
Principal Judge, Family Court, South East, Saket, New Delhi
to Family Judge-cum-Principal Judge at Nainital (Ramnagar
Camp), Uttarakhand. 
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner has stated before this
Court that there are two other cases pending between the same
parties at Ramnagar, Nainital and the respondent is appearing
in both the matters.
3. Learned counsel for the respondent vehemently opposed the
prayer for transfer. His contention is that the mother of the
petitioner  is  working  in  the  office  of  Sub-Divisional
Magistrate and therefore, she can influence the divorce
proceedings.
4. In the considered opinion of this court, as two cases are
1

--- Page 375 ---
already pending at Ramnagar, Nainital, Uttarakhand and the
respondent is attending both the cases, the present transfer
petition deserves to be allowed.
5. Accordingly, we allow the transfer petition and direct
the transfer of H.M.A. No. 610 of 2025, titled as  “Kumar
Abhishek vs. Manisha Ratnakar” , pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court, South East, Saket, New Delhi to Family
Judge-cum-Principal  Judge  at  Nainital  (Ramnagar  Camp),
Uttarakhand. 
6. Let the record of the case be transferred without delay.
7. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned court for
compliance. If video conferencing facility is available with
the transferee Court, the benefit of the same shall be
extended to the parties on such dates when their physical
appearance is not required. It is made clear that this Court
has not expressed any opinion on the merits of the matter.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
  
    
  …………………………………………………………J.
                   [SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA]
      …………………………………………………………J.
    [NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
NEW DELHI;
14th July, 2026

O R D E R
1. In spite of service of notice, there is no appearance on
behalf of the respondent.

Matrimonial Case No. 1093 of 2025, titled as “Rishab Kumar
vs. Smt. Nigam” , pending before the Principal Judge, Family
Court, Moradabad, Uttar Pradesh to the North-West District,
Family Courts, Rohini, Delhi.

Court that the petitioner is having a young daughter and she
does  not  have  financial  means  to  attend  the  case  at
Moradabad, Uttar Pradesh. Other grounds have also been raised
in the matter.

Court is of the considered opinion that the present transfer
petition deserves to be allowed.

the transfer of Matrimonial Case No. 1093 of 2025, titled as

--- Page 378 ---
“Rishab Kumar vs. Smt. Nigam” , pending before the Principal
Judge, Family Court, Moradabad, Uttar Pradesh to the North-
West District, Family Courts, Rohini, Delhi.

appearance is not required. It is made clear that this Court
has not expressed any opinion on the merits of the matter.
7. The  transferee  court  after  the  case  is  received  on
transfer, shall issue fresh notices in the matter enabling
the parties to appear. The transferee Court is directed to
list the matter in the month of August, 2026. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

respondent-Intervenor  has  placed  reliance  on

documents along with which additional documents have
been filed duly supported by an affidavit of the
respondent-intervenor.
1

--- Page 390 ---
It has been pointed out by the respondent-
intervenor that while the matter was pending before the
Delhi High Court, on the suggestion of Mr. Sanjay Jain,
the then Additional Solicitor General, appearing before
the Delhi High Court, a joint meeting was held in which
the respondent-intervenor, Mr. Sanjay Jain, the then
Additional Solicitor General and the Commissioner of
Income Tax (Judicial) had participated and deliberated
and the respondent-intervenor had been provided four
lists (duly verified) of his pending bills, which are
attached to the application (IA No.161808 of 2026) on
page nos. 217, 218, 219 and 56. 
According  to  the  respondent-intervenor,  the
lists were prepared after double review meetings held
between him, respondent-intervenor, learned the then
Additional Solicitor General and the Commissioner of
Income Tax (Judicial).
According  to  the  respondent-intervenor,  the
total  amount  of  these  four  lists  comes  to
Rs.1,37,00,000/-approximately,  and  that  these  four
lists do not cover the bills for which payment has
already been received by the respondent-intervenor.
These four lists are exclusively of the unpaid bills.
Mr. N.Venkataraman, learned Additional Solicitor
General, appearing for the petitioner, submits that
SLP(C)  No(s).  13113/2017
2

--- Page 391 ---
copy of the said application  (IA No.161808 of 2026) is
not available with him.

the said application to the petitioner during the
course of the day.

within two weeks.

                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsels for the parties.

--- Page 396 ---
2
2. Learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner  submitted  that  the
petitioner is ready for mediation to give a final quietus to the
present issue between the petitioner-husband and the respondent-
wife.
3. Learned counsel for the respondent-wife submitted that as per
his instructions, mediation has failed due to the attitude of the
petitioner-husband. However, he submits that the Court may give one
more opportunity for mediation.
4. Having regard to the aforesaid, the matter is referred to the
Supreme Court Mediation Centre (SCMC).
5. Initially, the parties are required to appear physically
before the Coordinator of the SCMC on 28.07.2026 at 02:30 p.m.
Thereafter, if a prayer is made by any party for conducting the
proceedings through video conferencing, the learned Mediator shall
take a call on the same.
6. Learned Mediator is requested to submit a report within a
period of two months thereafter. 
7. List on 07.10.2026.
8. At this juncture, learned counsel for the respondent-wife
submits that despite there being an order passed by the Family
Court to pay Rs.7,000/- (Rupees Seven Thousand) per month towards
maintenance by the petitioner-husband, there are huge arrears.
9. Having regard to the aforesaid, let the petitioner pay a sum
of Rs.2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lakhs) upfront, within two weeks to
the respondent-wife. Further, as we have been informed, some amount
had been deposited by the petitioner before the High Court. In view
thereof, the respondent shall be entitled to withdraw the same.

--- Page 397 ---
3
10. In the meantime, to facilitate the mediation, the execution
proceedings in Execution Petition (Crl.) No.138 of 2024, pending
before the learned Judge, Family Court, Rohini (North), New Delhi,
shall remain stayed.

IA No. 3124/2025 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE
DEFECTS
IA No. 3125/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Rauf Rahim, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Sharanagouda Patil, Adv.

    Ms. Tania Tamanna, Adv.
                   Mrs. Supreeta Patil, Adv.
                   Mr. Kotresh A. M., Adv.
                  For M/s S-legal Associates, AoR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Ms. Tarannum Cheema, AAG
    Ms. Patil Rekha Chandra Gouda, AoR
    Mr. Rajesh Inamdar, Adv.
    Mr. Akash Singh, Adv.
    Mr. B.L.N. Sanjit, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. C.M. Angadi, Adv.
                   Mr. Rameshwar Prasad Goyal, AoR                 
                   
         

--- Page 399 ---
2
                             O R D E R
Heard  Mr.  Rauf  Rahim,  learned  senior  counsel  for  the
petitioner,  Ms.  Tarannum  Cheema,  learned  Additional  Advocate
General for the respondent no.1 and Mr. C.M. Angadi, learned
counsel for the respondent no.2.
2. The matter was heard at length.
3. The Court was in a position to take a final view in the
matter. In the past, there had been mediation between the parties
for settlement of all outstanding issues between them but the same
had failed on the ground of there being no agreement with regard to
the amount, which the petitioner would give to the respondent no.2.
4. The Court taking a holistic and overall view in the matter
especially, the fact that on the one hand, the petitioner is
married and has two children and on the other hand, the respondent
no.2 is a lady, besides there being continued enmity between the
parties, which was likely to result in further litigation in future
also, deems it appropriate to try for one final round of mediation
in the Supreme Court itself.
5. Accordingly, the Court called upon learned counsels for the
parties to suggest the name of a person to whom the parties be
referred for mediation.
6. The parties agreed that the matter be referred to Mr. Sanjay
R. Hegde, learned senior counsel of this Court.
7. Accordingly, the parties are referred to mediation.
8. Mr. Sanjay R. Hegde, learned senior counsel is requested to
accept the responsibility.

--- Page 400 ---
3
9. Initially,  the  parties,  with  prior  consultation  with
Mr. Sanjay R. Hegde, learned senior counsel/Mediator, shall appear
before him through video conferencing. Thereafter, Mr. Sanjay R.
Hegde, learned senior counsel/Mediator shall fix the modality of
the mediation, including the date and place of mediation and
whether the parties are required to physically appear before him.
10. List on 16.09.2026.
11. In the meantime, to facilitate the mediation, all  inter se
proceedings pending between the parties shall remain stayed.
12. Learned counsels for the parties shall brief Mr. Sanjay R.
Hegde, learned senior counsel/Mediator and also provide him with
all relevant materials.
13. Before parting, we make it clear that all issues between the
parties, including the issue of respondent no.2 staying/occupying
the house belonging to the mother of the petitioner, shall also be
sorted out during such mediation.
14. We  further  request  Mr.  Sanjay  R.  Hegde,  learned  senior
counsel/Mediator for expediting the mediation.
15. Interim order passed by this Court on 24.01.2025 to continue
till the next date of listing.
(SAPNA BISHT)                                   (ANJALI PANWAR)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
